All right, just as people get added here, we'll go over a couple of housekeeping things. So everyone who sent a question in, when I say sent a question in, many of you sent mini chapters from War and Peace. So thank you for that. I'm going to go through, we have seven questions that were received in advance. I'm going to go through six of those, I think. And then the last one really is a client services question. So what I'm going to do with that, just to be fair to everybody, is I'll answer the first six questions that are more specific to this call, you know, for the core traffic engine training, I will then answer any questions that are submitted on the call. And if we still have time after that, I'll answer that the last question that's more of a client services call, but then it's fine if we have time. It'll just be a matter of where that fits. I want to make sure that today's call is as broadly useful as possible for everybody. What do you think, Andre, when I dive in, get started? Just let's do it. Yeah. So just for everybody, so everybody knows from a panelist perspective, obviously I'm on the call, Andre's on the call. Lauren is also on the call. And many of the questions that are related to Facebook, that you will do to perspectives will get Lauren's perspective and my perspective. Andre will always chime in with his perspective whenever he feels like it. The, you know, Lauren, just to clarify, Lauren is a far, far, far more skilled Facebook tactician than I am. And when I say tactician, I don't mean that in any derogatory sense. What I mean is that he is in the trenches of Facebook. I'm doing the work, knows it far better than I do. I'm much more of a Facebook strategist. So I think it's very helpful to have both of those perspectives. So whenever there's a question that's related to Facebook, we'll take two of those perspectives and just layer them on together. All right. Anything to throw out there before we get started, Andre? I don't really have anything to add. It's just sitting there drinking your coffee, like, whatever. I am actually not putting espresso, yeah. Of course. All right. One apology in advance. I will mispronounce some of your names. I'm just going to do first names. I apologize. I don't, it is what it is. I'll do my best. Let's dive in. So first question. Actually, I'm not even sure I'll identify people's questions. That way I can't screw up your name or identify names. But here's the first question. I'm going to read, excuse me, I'm going to, there are no one, I don't think anybody, maybe one person actually submitted one question. Almost all of you submitted many questions and some with background and context. So I'm going, I want to make sure everybody hears all of that. So for this first question, I'm just going to read what was submitted. There are three different things. Once we get to the end of it, we'll go back to the beginning. And Andre, I think you're going to want to chime in on this one too. All right. So first question. Can you please elaborate more specifically with examples if possible, how we can utilize the work we did for Value Proposition Canvas as we're writing our ad copy? How do the theme, lead and hook in the ad copy relate to the jobs, pains and gains? Canvas was hard, but great work. And I would love to make the most of it as I'm writing my ad copy. Then most of my audience is on Instagram. Can I use all of your recommendations for Facebook? Also for Instagram ads? I'm actually regarding the copy length and content. And then if I have a multi-page pre-sell site of three pages and third page being the opt-in, followed by delivering the lead magnet and soap opera sequence series, should I set my goal and ad manager as leads or sales? So lots of questions here. Let's start with the beginning. So Value Proposition Canvas is not, you're not trying to create like a template. That's what I want to be really clear about here. What you're trying to do is uncover the big levers in a market sub-segment or a maybe a markets segment. In most, what we think of as markets are often collections of markets. I think I may have used this example in the traffic engine. I can't remember specifically, but I worked with a client that had an analytics software package and they thought they had one, they really thought they had one audience and they marketed to one audience. But when we did the Value Proposition Canvas, it was very clear they had three. And what made it so clear was that the jobs, pains and gains, particularly the pains, were different for three different sub-segments of their larger audience. So that's really what you're looking for here is you're trying to find that match between and it doesn't have to be jobs, pains and gains. It could just be a very specific job somebody needs to get done. There's a very specific need. And one of the reasons that Value Proposition Design is so impactful is that you realize jobs have different, they're different expressions of jobs or functional jobs. That's what we tend to think of as a job. But they're also emotional and social components of jobs. And often the emotional and social need is far more important. Then the functional need. The example I teach a college class in entrepreneurship and the example I use, I like to make fun of myself all the time in the class. Students seem to enjoy that for some reason. But I drive a full-size Toyota Tundra and the functional need that truck serves, I live four miles from pavement so I do need a four-wheel drive truck. Excuse me, I live in a place with winter. So there's a functional need. I need to go from point A to point B. But I have the most expensive trim line that the Tundra comes in. That's the TRD Pro. Like it's $20,000 more than a base Tundra. So that doesn't, there's no functional need that that's, I mean, sure, I could tell myself there is. You can make up something about how I need the extra suspension travel or something. That's nonsense. There's an emotional need and I certainly don't want to dissect this on the psychiatrist couch here, psychologist couch to figure out what weird things are driving my decision making. But that's what the Jobs, Pains and Gains tries to uncover. Is if you try to sell me in this example, a basic Toyota Tundra, and you say, listen, it gets you from point A to point B, it's got the same four-wheel drive, you'll save all this money, that doesn't resonate with me. Whereas if you were to sell in a similar example, when my wife buys a car, all of that, the savings, the value that she get, like all of that really matters to her. That her emotional need for a vehicle, very different than mine, in many ways, pull her opposites, which is always a treat. So what you're trying to uncover when you do the value proposition canvas is not so much the theme, the lead, the hook, which you're really trying to narrow in on, is what is the thing that moves that audience, like just moves them at their heart and soul, that it's the thing that's more important than anything else. And the value proposition canvas is the framework to get those ideas out of your head and start assembling them. So then you can start pressure testing them. Is what often happens is somebody goes into a market and they think, let's just say it's a market of 10,000 people, and they go into a market of 10,000 people, and they've done the value proposition work, and they have identified 10 jobs in 10 pains, in 10 gains, and their messaging talks about 30 different things. And there's no one in that audience that cares about those 30 different things. It just doesn't work that way. But perhaps within that audience of 10,000, there is a subset of 3,000 that are really motivated by one of those jobs. And at the expense of everything else, if they could just get that one thing is so impactful. So that's what you would focus your ad. That would be the theme of your ad. It's that one thing you're always reminding yourself, am I speaking to the thing that actually matters here? And it's so, that sounds so obvious. I can't tell you how often I've seen in 22 years. I'm excited to say 22 years. It was November 3rd, when I started my 23rd year in business. So in 22 years, I can't tell you how many times I've seen people forget what it was they were actually trying to accomplish and what they're advertising. It sounds so obvious, but you're looking at it, like, I don't understand what you're selling here. Like, what is this about? You realize someone along the way just kind of forgot. And that's really what that's the beauty of the value proposition canvases to get it out of your head, what you think are the main movers in your for your audience, realize that probably when you find five things that you really think matters, let's say it's five jobs that really matter, those five jobs don't matter to everybody in your audience, which means you have smaller audiences within the audience. And then you need to speak to those smaller audiences very specifically. And that's where you get these really high leverage results. So I'm not sure I want to make sure that's the best answer here. So, Andre, any, any thoughts to add to this? You use value proposition canvas, we both use it. So any thoughts to add there about how that relates to this particular conversation? Well, you know, we, you and I interviewed one of our best friends yesterday, Jonathan Boyd. And so I guess that that's something we're going to be sharing on Friday as we do this recording now. And so we can certainly link to it in the show notes for, for, for this call. It's not something that we can quickly unpack how Jonathan approached figuring out what that thing is that has market like really want deep down like this emotional thing that's, that's driving them forward to, that just pulls them forward to take action. And for Jonathan, and you know, this is slightly out of context, but for him, it was the ultimate lead guitar light bulb moment. And, and that's how he packaged up that idea. But there's, there's copy, there's, there's ad copy and then landing page copy that unpacks that idea. And by the time they get to the bottom, that, that idea is packaged up in a way that just drives them crazy. So, you know, for him to all the work that he did to uncover that is the stuff that's inside of the Evalu proposition canvas, it's, it's talking to prospects and customers on the phone, which was, you know, there was one of the main things that he did. So that should not be understated that the Evalu proposition canvas is, is a framework that you can do the work, but it doesn't replace speaking to humans, you know, at every opportunity, whenever possible. There should be you know, it's important to, to speak to people that represent your market. And, you know, have those conversations because there are nuances that is, that they're going to say that won't reveal itself in, in any other way. So I think those two things that are, you know, when you overlap those, those two ideas together, the, the very proposition canvas framework, the idea of speaking to people on the phone and asking them questions. There's a, there's a really good book called The Mom Test. Just do a search on Google. I think it's the momtest.com. You can go to the website you can download the PDF, I think it's free or you can go to Kindle and just and purchase the book there. And that's really all the different questions and how to think about and how to think about asking, you know, real love people, how to, how to start to unpack those, those nuances that are in their, in their head and they not, and they don't necessarily know what they are, you know, they, they can't necessarily articulate it clearly, you know, well, by just asking simple questions. So you kind of got to, got to dig around a bit to reveal those things. So I think that's all I've got to say. But when we'll, we'll, we'll point this recording to the call that we did yesterday with Jonathan. And I think it'll add a nice nuance to, to this. And I'll just all add one last thing before we move on to the next question, which is just by way of example, just to kind of give you an idea of what you're looking for. Because I oftentimes the value proposition canvas is a gold mining exercise, right? It's a, it's a framework that captures thinking. That's what it directs thinking, but it also captures thinking and it helps you organize your thinking and to realize that there, you're talking about different things. When you talk about something that is a pain, that is different than something that is a gain, which is different than something, a job that somebody's trying to accomplish. And you don't, you're not mixing those things in together into this sort of incoherent soup with 50 different bullets of benefits and it's overwhelming and people just glaze over, we've all done that. So the framework is the, the idea is really to, to give you a place to collect and organize and then pressure test ideas. One of them that I'll, I'll share that'll kind of help you understand how this, how this leads to insight. The client that I mentioned before, when we discovered that they had three markets, one of those markets, and you can imagine this is pretty obvious in retrospect that one of the markets that would use a software analytics package would be agencies. Like mine. So I was, this was very close to me. However, the thing that, if you're an agency, the thing that you learn very quickly, if you run an agency or if you're a service provider that you learn very, very quickly is clients want reports. I don't know why I don't, it baffles me because I very, most of them have no idea how to interpret the data in a report. Most of the data that you can report is not really actionable at the system level. Like it makes no sense to me, but after 22 years, I still haven't figured this out, but it's true. Clients want reports. And, and that's really one of their biggest desires that they want to see a report and a PDF, like this thing that they get that they can share and point to. And this particular software package did not create reports. You couldn't create reports. And it was better to be clear. It was better because of that. But when you have a subset of your audience where the, perhaps the biggest pain they have, as it relates to the work they're trying to accomplish, is that their clients need reports and you're selling them something or you're trying to sell them something that does not create reports. It doesn't do the thing that you need more than anything else. That's a pretty tough sell. And if it's left unanswered, people just don't buy it. Agencies don't buy the product or they buy it and they're very unhappy later when they're like, well, wait a minute, how do I create reports for my clients? You say, well, you can't, but, and that was happening a lot. So value proposition canvas was the architecture was the framework that helped us really see that, that if we're going to go to this audience and this audience has this, this primal need for this particular thing and we don't do it, we need to either say in advance, we don't do that, we're not for you, or we need to figure out a way to do it, or we need to have a very clear alternative. That's really what value proposition canvas does. It shines lights on things like that and made it clear that that was just one subset of what they thought was one audience. All right, talking about this a lot. I don't want to give them a chance to take over for a while here. So second question. Sorry, Sean. There's just a quick question from Kieran just in the comments about what you said just early on. So he wants to know if he should hang on or not? I mean, well, sure. I mean, I think we're going to go over a lot of things that are valuable on the call. So in from that sense, yes, I think you it's valuable for you to spend time on the call and listen to other people's questions from the perspective of is your question going to get answered. We have seven questions so far. We haven't seen anybody else drop any other questions in our track record so far as we've gone three hours every time. So probabilistically speaking, I think we're going to cover that seventh question. Or I could just say yes, we'll cover it. So yes, we'll cover it. Okay, next question. Most of my audience is on Instagram. Can I use all of your recommendations for Facebook also for Instagram ads, especially regarding the copy length and content? So I'm going to have one from tactical perspective. Lauren can answer this far better than I can. But from a functional perspective, from a strategic perspective, Instagram is not Facebook. Clear and simple. You don't go to Facebook with the same intent that you go to Instagram. Your mindset's not the same. The infrastructure is not the same. What you get when you're on Instagram is not at all the same as what you get on Facebook. So no, from my perspective, not even close. I'm very curious to hear Lauren's perspective. And Lauren and I, just to be clear, we don't share these questions in advance. We disagree with each other sometimes. And I say disagree. We have different perspectives on things and that's part of the deal. There's no canonical right answer to these questions. Most of the time. Some questions there are. Most of the time there isn't. So oftentimes you have different perspectives and listen to both of those perspectives and find the one that resonate with you. So Lauren, take it away. So years and years ago, liking Facebook pages was really popular. Now, people just don't, it seems like people are liking pages less and less often. But people tend to follow pretty liberally on Instagram. So when someone says, my audience is on Instagram, that doesn't necessarily mean if you use paid traffic that your audience is not on Facebook. It just means that for whatever reason, organically, you've got a lot of followers on Instagram. Maybe you have a lot of engagement. I've seen it where people have tons of followers. They don't have a lot of engagement and they think their audience is on Instagram, but it's really just people who followed them a year ago and never see them or care about them. So I normally encourage people give Facebook a shot as well. Obviously, the platform is the same. The Facebook ads platform is how you place Instagram ads. So you can do them at the same time. But I would encourage people, yeah, don't leave out Facebook. It is a powerful platform to get in front of your audience. And even though you may think you may have more engagement on it, even if you really do, you can still get in front of those people through paid traffic on Facebook in a very powerful way. However, if you did want to optimize your ads for Instagram, I would start off just kind of thinking about Facebook and letting Instagram kind of be to the side. But if you wanted to optimize for Instagram, shorter copy is needed on Instagram. You can only get so many characters. I forget whether it's like 2200 or something, but you can only, it's very few words compared to what you can do on Facebook. So your copy will need to be kind of moderate length as opposed to longer copy that a lot of times we advocate. You can still pack a lot into that, but it will need to be a little edited. And then if you preview it on Instagram, you'll notice that your intro does not appear in the exact same way. You don't have the same amount of words that make it what the user sees as they're scrolling. So your intro will have to be even shorter to pack in what you want to pack in so that the user sees it while they're scrolling. Also, Instagram obviously is very image heavy and very video heavy. Facebook is that way also, but Instagram is even more so. So the importance of your image to stop the scroll or to communicate information is going to be even more important. So testing that's going to be even more important. And then finally, videos. You are limited, I think it's a minute or two minutes. I think it's two minutes currently that you're limited for a two minute video on Instagram, which sounds like, you know, that's a lot of time and it is, but you know, one client I'm working with, they're best performing videos like over 20 minutes. So with all those caveats, that's how you'll need to adapt is mainly just those tactical Instagram limitations. But other than that, I would just advocate, try both and see what works. Cool. Thanks, Lauren. That's such a much better and more nuanced answer. All right. Last question from this original series. I have a multi-page presale site of three pages. The third page is the opt-in page followed by delivering the lead magnet and soap opera sequence series. Should I set my goal on ads manager as leads or sales? This is a really important question. And it's an important question, sort of in what it implicates, because what we optimize for, this is the way I used to think about this was wrong. The way it's the way I've captured it in the way we've captured it and the traffic engine is correct. But in the past, it was very easy because of how we think about our, the structure of what I'll just call it, say it a funnel, just I don't love that term, but we'll just use that because we all know what it means. But there's a logical sequence in a funnel, in a process. And because of that, we make the mistake of thinking that the Facebook algorithm somehow reflects that logic. So that when somebody, but let's just say for the sake of argument that you can buy something before you buy it, you need to opt in. And before you opt in, you have three pages that you, that you read. And before you read that, there's an ad. So you could make the case like, well, I need, let me, first I'll optimize to get people to go from my ad to my landing page, because that's the first step. So why wouldn't I do that? And then I could, maybe then I could optimize for people who read, or for people who opt in, because that's the next chain in the next link in the chain. And then, well, at that point, then I could also optimize for people who purchase, because you can see the logical chain of progression there. But from Facebook's perspective, each of those things that I've just described are discreet. They're not linked. So that Facebook knows if you're somebody who clicks on a lot of stuff, you may also be someone who buys, but that doesn't, the fact that you click doesn't also mean because the click is the precursor to buying, that you're more likely to buy. It just means you're more likely to click. And then when you opt in, we, we think that because you have to opt in before you can buy in most situations, that opting in is, is a behavior that indicates you're more likely to buy later. From Facebook's perspective, those are discreet entries. Lots of people opt in for free stuff. So when you optimize for opt in, Facebook is going to look at the pool of people inside your audience who have exhibited that behavior in the past. Same is true for purchasing. So in an ideal world, you want to look at your actual end goal and optimize for that. Where the challenge comes in is the number of, of events that need to happen in a certain period of time to give Facebook enough data. And that, that's what requires us to make these decisions. In an ideal world, you would say, I am going to optimize for sales. The two things that can create issues with that. And I'm sure by the time we, we finish, there'll be like 30 things, but the two things that can immediately create issues with that are, do you have enough sales in a week to get the benefit of the Facebook algorithm and, or do people buy in a timeframe that is short enough that it feeds into Facebook's algorithm? So there was a, you know, 28 days is the maximum. And I know Facebook changed that for a while. I went to seven and Lauren can speak to sort of where the current state of affairs is with that. So if it's a very straightforward process, if someone sees your ad, read your content, opts in, sees an offer and they do it yes or no, and it all happens in within like a 24 hour period. Okay. Like then you don't have as many concerns, then it's about volume. But if you have a multi, if you have the multi-part pre-sell site, if you have a long soap opera sequence in between, and you start extending the period of time that it takes somebody to convert, then that muddies the signal a little bit. And what we're really trying to do is give Facebook a very clear signal. Lauren is 10 still, do you still feel like 10 conversions per week per ad set is somewhere in that neighborhood feels right? Or where do you think the thinking is the current thinking on conversions per week per ad set? I've heard like 50 per week is what they want. 10 per day. I'm sorry. Yeah. 10 per day. 10 per day is what some people advocate. Also, I've heard 50 per week, which is obviously about seven a day. There's not, there's no like limit. It's not like you get 49 conversions in a week and Facebook doesn't know what's going on. And then all of a sudden you get that extra one to bump you up to 50 and Facebook optimizes and it's just, you know, a golden rainbow. It is, you just try it and you see if it works, because sometimes Facebook will be okay at 20. Sometimes they may need more up to 50 a week. Yeah. So that to return to the actual question is we've told you a lot of things that now, if I were in your position, I'd be scratching my head saying, well, I still don't know exactly what to do. So it's, I think of it as a series of gates that you have to go through. So the first gate that you have to go through at the ad set level, where am I likely to get five to 10 ish conversions per day at the ad set level? So if you have the ad spend to do that and it sales that do that, often that's prohibitive. You don't have enough ad spend to get there. The next, the logical conclusion for that is to then to optimize for leads. And I think, yeah, it's hard. I want to be cautious of it. And it sounds like I'm overthinking this and really focusing too much on the nuance, but leads is a challenge, from my perspective, leads is a challenge because so much of digital marketing is based on this, what I think is a flawed model of lead generation. So Facebook has enormous amounts of data on people who become leads and opt in for free stuff. The entire model, internet marketing is built on the foundational model of offer some increasingly attractive whiz bang video series, PDF, ebook, you name it, keep ramping up what you're giving away for free, which creates really, really big audience segments of people who download free stuff and download free stuff isn't your business model, I don't think. So I don't love lead optimization. I think it, if you have a better alternative, it is what it is. Just, I think really you have to be aware of what you're saying to Facebook and what Facebook is doing in return. And what you're saying to Facebook in this situation is I want you to go find, you like just, I'm going to make up a small number of an audience, but this isn't what you would do, just imagine it's bigger. There's a hundred, let's assume there are 100,000 people in this audience that you've identified through a set of characteristics you think those are, that's my tribe, or more accurately, my tribe is in that group of 100,000 people. And you say, okay, I think my ad budget will allow me to get five to 10 leads per day so I can get the full benefit of the Facebook algorithm and the lead is the precursor to the sale therefore because I'm getting leads, I'm also getting people who are likely to buy because you can't buy if you don't opt in. That makes perfect sense. That's not how Facebook sees it. And Facebook isn't going out of their way to tell you that's not how they see it. They actually want you to think it works that way because that you're more likely to do it. And I'm not suggesting anything nefarious. It's just, it's a little bit, I believe, of an intellectual sleight of hand. And what you're doing instead in that scenario is you're saying to Facebook, here's this pool of 100,000 people, I believe my people are within that I can afford to get five to 10 leads per day. And I want the benefit of the algorithm. So I'm going to do that. And what they're going to do is go find within that group of 100,000 people, people who do probably the most common conversion event in internet marketing, which is they become leads. So knowing that, choose wisely, I guess is my message. So and then the question becomes, what's the alternative? What else could you do? Well, again, it's all within the confines of five to 10, five to 10 conversions per day. So it could be a custom conversion that they see page three, if that's what it takes. And you have a three part series, it could be leads. Ideally, it would be sales. Just know what you're getting in return for the input that you're putting in. If I, to bring this to a close, if I had this exact same scenario, and I had enough ad spend that I couldn't quite get enough sales, like maybe I was getting three to five sales per day, I think I would, Lauren might talk me out of this, but I think I might still then just optimize for sales and hope that I was giving Facebook enough data, even though it was a less pronounced signal, it was a more valuable signal. I would, I would probably lead in that direction. So we spent a lot of time in this first question. This is a great question. These are foundational things that we need to talk through. So I really appreciate that question. All right, let's jump on to the second question. Yeah, go ahead. I've got some, some dumb questions related to that. Just playing devil's advocate here for a second. So I'm guessing it could be possible that, so the very first email that goes out on the separate sequence, let's just say for argument's sake, to keep it simple that there's a PDF download, which is the, which is the promise lead magnet. Presumably, it's possible to, when, if anybody clicks that link to download that PDF it triggers this conversion process and which is still a lead. So that lead would be different to somebody that's just opted in, which would be different to somebody that's hit page, you know, that's gone past page one, for example, would, would Facebook treat that lead, which is obviously harder to get than somebody that's, that's just made it past page one, for example, or who's just managed to opt in, but they don't download, you know, they don't click the link to download the, the thing, which is a signal that somebody that's motivated to make a purchase in terms of just, you know, what are the signals going to be? It's probably somebody that's, that's going to be doing everything, clicking the links. Would that lead be slightly better? So you'd get less of them, but then Facebook's algorithm would think, actually, this is, this is weird, because this person's getting less leads than everybody else in the pool that we're sending them. Maybe we should send them better leads. I'm just using maybe bad language, but does that process, do you think it happens where if you make that lead as hard as possible, as difficult as possible, that it becomes over time a better lead that they will send you? Wouldn't be a lead in the accustomed conversion. And I was actually, that was an example I was thinking about before you said that. So I double that I doubles advocated myself and then you double advocating me. So I like double, double. So I have a good friend, Jeremy Boston run strike point media, they do lead gen at scale, just staggering amounts of lead gen and one of the distinctions Jeremy makes, which I think is a really important distinction. And I don't know what their naming convention is, but I've named it a qualified lead. So instead of optimizing, simply for leads, they look at what percentage of those leads actually consume the content, go to the next thing. So I think in this scenario that Andre just presented, and this is, this is certainly a way to think about doing it, is you could have, let's say, email number one fulfills on the promise of the opt-in. So you opt in for something, you confirm that you've opted in, you've opted in. And now what, and let's say the promise that you made was a three part mini course that is delivered over email. So it's hard to track from that perspective, but you still want to signal that somebody is qualified in some way. So rather than have it just delivered by email, I might in that first, the first email that they get, it may be the second or the third, depending on how far you want to move it down the line, to amp the quality up. But I might have, I might not put the content in the email itself. And instead, they get the email, hey, thanks so much for signing up. This is what you're going to get. We really appreciate having you here to access your XYZ, click here. Then when they click that, that fires the conversion event pixel. And so yes, it's a, it's a more qualified lead than just saying somebody became a lead by virtue of opting in. Lauren, any thoughts on that? I mean, I think this is, we can kick the conversion event down the road really, as far as we want, really as far as we want to go. Do you have any thoughts about when that's smart to do that, when it's not smart to do that, where we can get ourselves into trouble doing that? Just riff on that for a minute. Yeah. So I would, there are custom events that you can do. You can make up any event you can call whatever you want to, so that say you've got a multi-page pre-sell, someone goes to page two, you can fire an event there that, you know, they click to page two. You can create a custom conversion from that event. Same for clicking to the next page. And then they opt in going to the email if they click to download. One thing about email is I'm always wondering if the pixel would fire, just depending on email clients and everything else like that, that would be kind of tricky potentially. But assuming that you could actually get the pixel to fire there, you could do that. What I would do was set up, there are standard events and there are custom events. Okay, so like a standard event is lead, purchase, these pre-determined events that Facebook has. Then there are custom events, which is you just create whatever you want to do. Then there are custom conversions, which is you saying out of all these events, I want you to uniquely track this particular event. What I would do is at the very beginning, set up whatever custom events you want to, set up whatever custom conversions you want to, even if you do nothing but track. For instance, on your, you could have your, you could fire a custom event and we do this for like multi-page pre-sell. You could fire a custom event once someone has been on the page for say eight seconds or something like that. And that would be indicative that the person's actually reading and actually consuming the content. You can fire those custom conversions all throughout the funnel to track everything you want to track. Then you could of course track the lead. You could track, do they open up the email and go to the lead magnet? You could track anything further. Do they go to the sales page for your product? Do they add to cart? Whatever you want to add into the future, do they spend two minutes on your sales page? Whatever custom conversions and custom events you can think of, I would just set all that up from the very beginning. And then that serves two purposes. Number one is you can track. So you can see in the Facebook dashboard, you know what, for some reason we've got a really big drop off here. Why is that? Is that expected? And what can we do to improve that if needed? And the second is because you have those custom conversions set up, Facebook is getting that data. Then you can use that later to optimize if you want to. Part of the problem with optimizing, if you're using like a custom conversion as opposed to us, if you're using a standard event just like lead, Facebook gets that. They're like, okay, I know what a lead is. I can optimize for the standard conversion. But if you have a custom conversion, Facebook might not know exactly what that looks like. What does this custom conversion mean? What type of people follow this and actually trigger this custom conversion? So the earlier you can set that up, the more data that's going to give Facebook, even if you're not optimizing for it now, Facebook is tracking that data so that you could have the option of optimizing for that later. In the case of StrikePoint, they can get away with that. They can use something like a custom qualified lead event because they're driving enough volume that Facebook can figure that out pretty quickly. If it's a smaller operation where they don't have that volume, you might want to go with a standard event to optimize for at the beginning so that Facebook gets it. Hopefully, that makes sense. But I guess, all in all, I echo what Sean says. This seems to be one of those perennial questions that gets asked so often. I understand because it's the question I asked and still ask myself so often. But the reality is what we're talking about is ultimately one human being selling to another. That's taking place over seeing an ad and clicking to a multi-page pre-sell and consuming that content and opting in and going to a sales page. There's so many psychological factors and communication that goes on in the midst of that. Trying to boil that down to just give it to the computer and let the computer do everything, it's much more complicated than that. I would totally go the approach that Sean advocated. Figure out where you got five to 10 per day of a conversion as far down the funnel as you can go. Figure out where you got five to 10 per day. Optimize for that. If it's working great, maybe dip your toe in the water and try a different event later. Just to give you an example, Jonathan Boyd, who Andre and Sean talked about earlier, he's sending traffic at one point, sent traffic to a multi-page pre-sell where they opted in for a PDF and then they would see an upsell or a VSL for a $27 product. He was optimizing for sales because he was doing enough volume that he was getting 20, 30, 40 sales a day. He could do that. I looked at that and I was like, or we talked about it and I was like, well, let's just try doing it as a lead because you've got this multi-page pre-sell. Your leads are pretty qualified. Let's just try optimizing for a lead and see how that works. We were able to reduce our lead cost significantly, but then our cost per purchase went up a lot. That's just a tribute to what Sean said, is that the Facebook algorithm, if you tell the Facebook algorithm to give you cheap leads, it's going to give you cheap leads that might not necessarily convert into purchases. But that's an example right there, Jonathan, where you can use a multi-page pre-sell, lead magnet, optimizing for purchases, and he's making it work. All right. That's great stuff. Thank you, Lauren and Andre. I'll just close that question with one sort of overall point. We'll be very clear about something. The traffic engine is, and I say this all the time, emails in the content. It's a learn how to fish, not here's a fish course because the here's a fish courses generally don't work for the overwhelming majority of people who go through them. The underlying assumption is that in the data shows that over 90% of people never complete courses. You can give something that works for a part of an audience knowing that most people aren't going to go through it anyway. There's no downside to the person who does that. From my perspective, that's just not ethical. It's not in line with my values. What we're trying to do here is get you in motion towards something, but also recognize that this is a learning how to think about the platform's course because there's so much nuance. If we just say do this, optimize for why we're doing your terrible disservice, it really does require this ability to take a step back and look and think, well, if I make this choice, this is what's actually happening and does that make sense for my business? Let's move on. We spent 44 minutes on three questions. Nice job, Sean. Second question. First, thanks for your awesome course. Thank you for the awesome compliment. This is a question we've heard in various forums lately. I'll read through the whole thing and then I'll answer this question, but it's a broader question that I'll answer at the same time. My Facebook account was restricted, but it's a little bit strange. Here's the context. I didn't advertise at all this year. He's in an ID confirmation in. He traveled. Facebook says they responded. The first part is we have somebody with issues accessing their Facebook account. I'll share both of the questions and then we'll go into the answers. That's part one. What happens? Why does this happen? What goes on? What's the deal with my Facebook account? Either me not having access to it or the account's been shut down. Whatever. There's that set of questions. Then the second one. I've created a new ad account, but I feel doing ads on Facebook is not a professional move. It's too unstable. There's no support. You can be banned for no apparent reason. He wants peace of mind in his business. He closes with you. Probably don't have any answers, but maybe. There's a third question too about a French agency that I'll answer at the end. Let me say this in plain language as possible. Working with Facebook sucks. Plain and simple. Facebook is miserable. I spent the overwhelming majority of by time and by dollars of my hands on in the trenches experience with advertising was with Google, not Facebook. Facebook is an entirely different animal. Google has policies that are published. If you violate those policies, there are ramifications. If you don't violate those policies, you're fine. Facebook has published policies. Those published policies are not complete. There are things that you can do that violate policies that you are not told. There are also people who are openly violating the policies that if you do the same thing, you will suffer ramifications that they are. The example I love to use is the Motley Fool financial newsletter having pictures of marijuana plants in their ads promoting a pot stock newsletter. It is a immediate direct crystal clear violation of Facebook's terms of service to show a picture of a marijuana plant in a Facebook ad. I know somebody who saw that the Motley Fool was doing it. They had a similar product. They advertised it. Their account, their ad account was banned. Their business manager account was banned and their individual access to Facebook was banned. The reason they were given was you violated our terms of service. You put a marijuana plant in a thing. We clearly say not to do that. That person's response was, but so does the Motley Fool and this ad has been running for years. The answer was, yeah, right? That is what it is. Facebook is not great to work with. I understand how that feels as an advertiser. I have woken up plenty of times for my business manager account restricted. I can't access client accounts. It happens. Sometimes, usually within so far, knock on wood. 24 to 72 hours later, it is reinstated. Sorry, it was an AI thing. It didn't mean you didn't do anything wrong. That sucks. That is a really hard way to run a business, especially when you are responsible for lots of clients. Then, wait, there is more. It got a lot worse recently because of the US presidential elections, because of the nature of the US presidential elections. Whatever your political persuasion is, it is fine. You do you. Because we had at least one of the two political parties, and depending on what you believe, we could say both political parties seem to have a really loose tolerance for the truth. Facebook has made some pretty sweeping decisions about political advertising. They have a lot of heat. We don't need to go into why all this happened. Facebook was miserable. Then, somebody took the dial that was on misery level three and spun it to misery level 30 because of the US elections, with accounts getting banned, support not being able to keep up, individual access being shut down, you name it. We can lament that reality, which is happy to do with you. We can all cry in our beers together because it sucks. Or, we can recognize that it platform, the Facebook platform for whatever reason does not seem to be particularly mature, and you still just approach it with integrity. That's my strategy. I know that's Lauren's strategy that we're going to be buffeted by winds outside of our control. You, when you advertise on Facebook, you as individuals, agencies, whatever, you're going to get knocked around by Facebook with things that are out of your control. The only thing that you have control over in that situation is how you act on the platform. If you try to bend the rules, if you try to get away with stuff, if you don't take the time to learn the rules and violate them, you most likely will not be able to use that platform. It just is what it is. It can't help you there. They're not particularly friendly about that. If you try to game the system and you get an ad account, a ban, you just create a new one, they will consider that a violation of their terms of use. If you do it enough times, you'll get banned as an individual from advertising on a platform, which is a real treat. If you try to create a new user profile, the more you try to get around it, Facebook knows massive amounts of data. They can figure this stuff out. The things over which we have control is how do we do our work. We're trying to do in the traffic engine is to say, here's where all the quicksand is, but more importantly, recognize it's a social platform, overwhelmingly social platform. We want to be good actors on that platform. We want to approach it with integrity. We don't want to call people out in ways that are negative. We don't want to call people out in ways that make them feel bad. We don't want to say things that are disingenuous. We don't want to overhype claims, even if we sold something and somebody got this incredible result, but that result was unusual. We're not going to talk about that. We're going to be cautious and conservative. We're going to use the platform in a way that reflects what the users of that platform expect in your individual audience. You can do all of that and still run into problems. That's the sad fact, but at least then, if you run into problems, you can have an honest conversation with a person in support, like chat or however you do that, and say, I've approached this platform as a good actor. I understand the terms of service. I'm doing my best to be a good actor on this platform. If they can't go find something that's a clear violation, the probability of your account being reinstated or your access being reinstated is much higher than if they go in there like, well, wait a second, you've got 50 disapproved ads that you never did anything with. If you think about it, a disapproved ad is a signal from Facebook back to you. Hey, this is a problem. You need to do something about it. You struck a match here. There's a candle burning. Now, there are 50 candles burning. Now, it's starting to look like a bonfire and you're not paying attention to it. So, guess what we're going to do? Shut off your access. We've got to listen to Facebook signals. I realize this doesn't, for all of you who've dealt with this, it's very small comfort. This is a miserable experience. If I could wave a magic wand and not have to advertise on Facebook, I would wave that magic wand at a heartbeat. But the problem is, and the opportunity is, that Facebook is a massive aggregation of audiences with very granular access and it can produce incredible results. So, we approach it knowing what we're in for. Lauren has done a masterful job in the traffic engine of explaining how to approach the support team when you've run into an issue. I encourage you to reread that section. Really do that. You've created a new ad account. So, I want to be cautious. I just want you to know that by doing that, you have technically violated Facebook's terms of service because it looks like you're trying to undermine their rules. So, that can create problems. So, I don't want to suggest that sunshine and unicorns, that by doing that, things are going to be okay. They might be. Whatever happened in the other account could be in error. And if you create a new account and you're a good actor and you do the right things, it may be fine, but we just don't know. And that the creating the second account may very well be viewed as trying to undermine the Facebook platform and sort of the approach. So, just proceed cautiously with that. As far as Facebook's too unstable, it's miserable. I don't want to do it. There's Google too. If Facebook makes you miserable, one of the reasons the traffic engine includes Google and Facebook, partly it's because of active intent with search versus passive potential interest with Facebook. They're wildly different platforms, but Google is a more mature platform and they've been around a lot longer. They have a lot better support staff and they're much, they enforce their rules much more clearly. And the rules that are enforced are the rules that you're given access to. There are no other rules. So, you don't violate things that you weren't made aware of. So, if Facebook just isn't your thing, go through the Google side of things, really max that out and then come back to Facebook later. Oh, nice. Yeah, I'm good. Coffee that I don't have. All right. Let's move on to the next question. So pretty short question. Two questions here. Actually, I thought there was one. So first question. Is it a good idea to run branding and engagement campaigns before a conversion campaign to warm up a cold audience and build a custom audience, which can then be used to create a lookalike audience for the conversion campaign? I love this question. So this, if we were having this conversation three years ago, three or four years ago, this would be a, like my answer would be so different. So three or four years ago, earlier than that, too, because there was so much you could do on Facebook and because it was so cheap to do those things, then everything that you've just described made so much sense. And because the algorithm was less mature. So you run a campaign to get whatever you engage with an audience, somehow they do a thing, like they click on something, you show them something that's intellectually interesting that would, and you can get super sophisticated with this, like this, you try to find something that's interesting to the subset of your audience that's most motivated, right? So an article or a headline, you really, you're trying to, you're not trying to appeal to everybody, you're trying to create this signal. And then you get them to engage with it. And he's, oh, okay, because they clicked on that thing, an article or a blog post that you created that was very specific to the niche within your audience. And I'll give an example of this in a second, so it makes more sense. And then because they did that, you then build, you warm up that audience, you show more content like that, and maybe you show them some videos and you're, and you're building rapport with them by with these interactions. And then all roads lead to Rome at some point, and you've got a warm audience now, and that warm audience you are showing your, your offer to, and that warm audience becomes the foundation of a look-alike audience that starts the whole process over again. Right. That worked when clicks were cheap, and the algorithm wasn't very smart. And it's still lots of people still do this. And so I want to be cautious how I, I can be, I'm very opinionated about that. I'm very opinionated in general, but I'm very opinionated about this particular idea. What I think it hides sometimes is it's a really, it's a really good way to do a few things. It's a really good way to feel like you're busy. It's a really good way to feel like you're making progress. It's a, it's, it's a great way to use the system, the system, the Facebook platform, and you can make it very sophisticated. It makes sense intellectually, but what I also notice it does is it, it allows people to have very fuzzy thinking. And I'm not suggesting this for the person who's asking the question, this is you at all. It's a very important question, and I appreciate it. So don't, this is not feedback to you to be very clear. This is feedback about how I've seen this play out in the world. But it becomes like, what I think about is, can you sit down with a sheet of paper and a crayon and show me how your business works? Straight, straight forward, right? It's like, here's an ad, here's a thing they buy here, or here's an ad, there's three pages, there's five emails, and an email four and five, they can click on a link to buy. Can you draw that for me on a piece of paper? And if you can, can you then go to Facebook and make that work? Just as bare bones as that. And can you create an ad that pre-qualifies so that before somebody clicks on the ad, there's a much higher percentage chance that they're the right prospect, because you've told them all the reasons why the wrong prospects should go away. If you can do that, then get them to the landing page. You've, you've really done most of what people try to do with these other very sophisticated contraptions is how I think about them. They make sense and they made it, they made a lot of sense, and I've done a lot of these to be clear. I mean, a lot of these for a long time, they made sense when traffic was cheap and the algorithm was dumb. They don't make much sense anymore when traffic is expensive and the algorithm is super smart or super capable. So here's an example of how I use this in the past. I had a client for a period of time who had a product for sales managers, not a sales person, a sales manager. It was hard to target sales managers on Facebook at the time. That may be different now. You could target selling professionals, broadly speaking, but not the sales manager. In her product, it was, it was the agency she worked with before and got her enormous amounts of engagement on at the ad level and the landing page level and then everything fell off a cliff. And the reason it fell off a cliff is they, is the everything they were doing was targeting sales professionals, big promises for sales professionals. But that's not who her audience was. So she's getting a false signal. So we, we went back and that the engagement campaign that we created was hyper focused on content that was only interesting to a sales manager. And we were very clear about that. So we got a much better signal. So that, I mean, that's kind of a way that you can use engagement on the front end, as long as you don't make the mistake of clouding and muddying the signal that you're getting, which is very easy to do. So sort of going all around this topic and I'm doing that because again, this is a teach the fish class. I want you to know how we think about these things. So the way I think about this is maybe, maybe you should do all of that, but you should not do all of that before you have demonstrated with clarity that you can go from A to B and sell your stuff. Because if you can't go from A to B and sell to the most responsive part of your market quickly from ad and ideally, and if, from my perspective, you, if you can't sell, let's say you had a $27 product, if you can't sell a $27 product from an ad to a landing page, I'm not sure I'd go too much beyond that until I could. When we put all these other pieces in, we just keep adding layers of abstraction that are, that we don't know if the thing, if we need to first prove that the thing we're trying to accomplish, we can accomplish. And that is the scariest thing in the world to do. And that's why people don't do it. It is terrifying to put yourself out in the world and say, I've created this thing that's valuable. And I'm going to put it out there and see if you think it's valuable too. And I'm going to let you make nasty comments about it. God knows that people make horrible comments on Facebook. I'm going to let you, I'm going to put myself out there and let you come back and say terrible things about me. I'm going to, I'm going to do that because I want to see if this value I've created is actually valuable. That is terrifying. So what often happens instead is we forget to prove that part of it. And we're like, well, we'll do this other thing and we'll kind of warm up an audience. And sometimes warm up again, this is not for the person asking the question, this is broadly speaking, sometimes warm up an audience means cross your fingers and hope they like you more. And they won't say bad things about you later. Right? That's, that's a lot of what's happening here because we're driven by this terrible fear that we're going to get judged for this value that we've put out into the world. And it takes enormous courage to act in the face of that fear and to stare at it and say, yep, it's probably, that's probably going to happen. I love what Todd Herman does. If you know, Todd Herman, he wrote the book, The Alter Ego, Ego Effect. He's done a bunch of other things in 90 to a year. But Todd Herman every month has a, one of the designers on his team, they make a poster, they hang in the office and Todd hangs one in his personal home office. He makes a poster of the nastiest comments that people left on Facebook. And these are really, really good comments. I will, this is a family friendly environment, I won't share any of them. But outstanding comments, just to, to take the, to have to laugh at it, right? Just to take that out of there. So long, this is a long circuitous answer to a short question. That's my favorite thing to do. But recognize, and this, this may be different for your situation, the person who asks the question, or those of you listening may say, but wait a second. And I think somebody just asked, okay, yeah, so somebody asked, what about a high ticket offer? So this is where the nuance gets really important. This is where we need to think about what are these other things that we're doing? How are they contributing? And the first question I would ask anybody, any situation, any dollar amount, have you proven the offer? Meaning, have you sold the offer online as you're presenting it? If the answer to that is no, don't do any of the other stuff, just figure out, can you sell it online? You've got to, you have to go through that gate first. You have to be able to sell this thing to another human being online. However, you're going to do that. Maybe you close by phone, if it's high tickets, fine. But you need to, you need to do the thing that is the ultimate thing that the system is supposed to do first. Then once you've shown that you can do that, that it, in some capacity, then it becomes a matter of what's additive here. Maybe it's additional campaigns in different audiences that are crafted the same way. Maybe it's changing the structure of the campaign. Maybe it's adding an engagement campaign on the beginning. In that situation, makes a lot of sense. Maybe it's doing, I mentioned this, I had a conversation with somebody earlier, maybe last week, about when to do a retargeting campaign when you don't want to get clicks, which not so important on Facebook because it's a CPM environment, but if you pay for clicks on Google's display network, it's kind of a funny thing to think about, like, why would you retarget and not want clicks? Well, if you're an expert in a field and you're, somebody comes to your site and they're not, they don't know that you're an expert in your field and you've, they've had some exposure to you and then all of a sudden they see you everywhere and they see you, there's no called action, they just see you positioned as an expert, that's very valuable, right? So campaigns like that can be additive. So returning to the original question through this lens of high ticket, yes, if you have sold this, start to finish A to B, you're selling it online and you have some level of success with that, then start working outward from that and say, okay, what would, what would amplify the results that I'm already getting? What, and if you're, if you own a sphere of influence, you know this idea of instilling beliefs, perhaps there are three or four things somebody needs to believe and internalize before they can accept your offer. So you could do an engagement campaign where you're covering those beliefs and it might be multiple touchpoints at that point, or it could be a single touch point where they come in and there's a whatever, maybe two or three videos or three or four pages of content where you're going, you're establishing these beliefs and that somebody who, let's say it's three pages of content, somebody who makes it to the third page in that engagement campaign might actually be the basis for a good lookalike audience to start over again. So yes, there are ways this can work, just don't let it be a reason to do a lot of things that generate heat but no light, meaning that a lot of things happen, but it doesn't move the needle, it doesn't make the boat go faster, right? That's my, the phrase that I love so much that's in the free paid traffic training is that everything is subordinated to making the boat go faster. And if the thing that you're doing, you can't in a very short period of time show how that thing is making the thing that you care most about happen more, then you have to be willing to let it go. That's a really long answer to the question. Lauren, thoughts to add on this? I think talk for an hour about it. No, I think it was great. I mean, it's funny, I was like thinking how I'd answer it and it was pretty much the same thing, you know, three years ago, two years ago, this was popular, people would do this, they'd put out a video and then they would retarget video views and that worked then because traffic was cheaper and exactly Sean said the algorithm wasn't as powerful as it is now. More and more now what people are having results with is just going direct for that, for what you want, you know, direct response instead of having to engage an audience first. In terms of, you know, high ticket, you can try it. I mean, it's something you could do, engagement and then you position them for the sale, but that you may find more success in a traditional kind of lead gen type of environment or maybe you're sending them to a webinar. And the reason then is you're getting their email address, there's some hand raising that they're doing already. There's some more relationship that they're engaging with other than simply just watching a video on Facebook and all of that may be conducive to you being able to engage with them more, particularly because you can email them as well. And that may help you secure that high ticket sale more, but all of this is just stuff that you can experiment with. But my gut would tell me that the tried and true models will probably be what is going to give you the most success. Lauren, there's a second question here and I'm going to let you answer this because I'm going to have a good answer to it. What's the best strategy when using a messages campaign objective? Any thoughts on that? Oh, okay. Yeah, so messages campaign, like, first of all, like be very careful whenever, if you have any automated follow up, a lot of times that's what people do with these messages, they'll put them in kind of an auto responder type of sequence. Be very careful with that because Facebook has rules about that. I'm actually not that familiar with those because they change all the time and I'm seeing pages that are getting restricted going back to our earlier conversation on compliance. I'm seeing all sorts of accounts and pages and people that are having issues because their chat sequence is non-compliant. So that's an entirely different issue. If you're talking about just messages in terms of talking with someone, I've just used it as a call to action. Just think of it like a phone call. Like, what type of, how could you get in front of someone and want to continue that conversation? I don't, I think of it, it's more of does this selling process, does this communication process require a conversation? Is that going to be the best way right now for me to have a one-on-one conversation? And if so, well then that would lead itself into a messages campaign. I don't start with saying, all right, I've heard someone say use a messages campaign. Now let me figure out how to do that. I think the type of campaign is dictated by the communication strategy that you want to have. So I've used messages campaigns before for like small, super small businesses that just didn't have the resources to even have landing pages. And it was just a way to, it was like a local gym, one of my friends' gyms, and he just wanted to get members. So it was just kind of like, hey, we've got these specials coming up. Do you want a message just to learn more? Then have good salespeople on the other side who understand the process of selling because they think that Facebook's going to do the magic. And I pretty much just send people like, all right, here's where you send your check. You know, that's not going to work. But all sorts of things you can do just to encourage a conversation in messages. There's no real unique strategy. Generally, it's, I wouldn't do anything goofy like some direct response, open loop. And then if you want to find out the answer, message us, no, I mean, that's really not going to give you success. It's more just, hey, we're selling a car, we're selling a red widget, whatever it is. If you want more information, we have these particular specials we can tell you about whatever questions you may have, reach out to us for more information. And sometimes that works. I've seen it work with like case study type stuff where you're like, hey, we had these really results with, we had these great results for this client. Do you want us to tell you about it? How it may be a fit for your business? Hit us up here on Messenger. Great stuff, Lauren. Thank you. All right. A quick pause to thank our sponsors. We don't have any sponsors, but I just, I want to take a moment to thank all of you who are on the call. Your, your attention, you know, we, Andre and I talk about this all the time. Your attention is the most precious commodity that you have. And the fact that you're sharing some of that with us, deeply appreciate that. So thank all of you. If you, if you hear things on today's call, a couple of things, there'll be a second, there will be another call as part of this cohort, any future calls that happen through the traffic engine, you have, you have lifetime access to it, all the updates, everything you'll always be able to be on them. If you hear something today and you want further clarification, just reply to an email, one of the weekly emails, and it gets to me, and I will reply to you there too. So just mentioning that as we transition to the next question. This is a short one and it's, we'll have a little fun with this. I love this question because I hear this a lot. So I would love to know if you have experience with YouTube ads, and if this is something that you recommend. So yes, done a lot of that YouTube advertising in the past. It is not something that was included in the traffic engine very deliberately, for a few reasons. One of the reasons is, a few, when I say a few reasons, I'm going to get up to 25 or so in a second, but one of the reasons is it's an entirely different approach, fundamentally from from the beginning to the end. It is much more of a volume approach. And also corollary to that, it is one that in my experience, not only benefits from significant budgets, but actually requires significant budgets. And when I say significant budgets, I mean, in my experience, nosebleed budgets. The larger reason why I didn't include it in the traffic engine is because everyone who I have spoken to who does YouTube well, like really well paid YouTube, they all have some variation of the same story. And that, that story distilled down, always said sound something like this, we spent about a half a million dollars on YouTube before we figured out how to make it work. And when I hear that, it's just let that sink in for a minute. First of all, it's not an exaggeration. These are companies that are spending millions and millions and millions of dollars, often millions of dollars a month to make their digital advertising work. And YouTube doesn't, there are, they all, the people who I've spoken to, and I've spoken at length to a lot of, with a lot of people who really understand this, they have sort of similar directionally relevant commonalities. Like one of the things you'll hear a lot is there are certain ways to target on YouTube that work in most ways don't. And they're, and then within that, of course, it's the first five seconds of the ad that is really selling people paying attention. And if you've ever spent any time on YouTube, you know that if that first five seconds doesn't grab you, then you're going to click skip ad. So there's, there's, there's certainly some things like that that we could have included in the traffic engine, we could have had sort of this broad overview and and brought people what I would consider 80% of the way out of potential quicksand and giving you a good starting point. And probably I'm talking myself into this now, which is always dangerous. This is something I do on Q&A calls, which I really wish Andre and I were closer in the same space for these calls. And I really, because what I would really like him to do is when I start going down with these roads, I would like him to punch me in the face before I finish talking, but he can't. So I'm not protected. And don't, don't get excited about the Andre because I know that for other reasons, sometimes he wants to punch me in the face. Having said that, I'm just making you really think about it. So I can imagine at some point in the future, in the next six months or so, that I might include a like a missing chapter or something about here's what I've learned from people who spend lots of money on YouTube. Here's what matters and put that as sort of a two or three page overview. I imagine that will happen because I've talked to some really smart people doing YouTube. But it's not, I don't want it to be part of the traffic engine because I don't think it's necessary. It's additive, sure, for some businesses, it could be very important. I get all of that. But I want you to get the active intent first. I want you to build on a rock solid foundation of people who are raising their hand saying, I am looking specifically for what you have to offer. Go do that. Do that really well. Exhaust that first. Really get like, get everything you can from that. Then go to the people who are like, no, I'm not even sure I'm interested in that, but I might be. That's Facebook. That's the GDN. That's other things. Then there's a lot to explore there too. YouTube, it just doesn't make the final cut for me. Now, if you're in a business that is just dependent on video and it's the only way that you can sell, I want to give something for that audience. And at the same time, I'm terrified of distracting anyone. It's like, oh, YouTube looks fun. I spend a lot of time on YouTube. I love to make videos. I'm not going to be at the level of advice that I would be from my experience elsewhere. And I do know I've seen six figure mistakes made on, actually, I've seen seven figure mistakes made on YouTube. I'm thinking about a particular client I worked with last year who, and this will frame, I'll end with this, this will frame the YouTube conversation, I think, in a nutshell. I was hired by a company last year, last summer, who was spending $2.7 million on Google. And when I heard that, and I knew, I'm sorry, $2.4 million, when I heard that, immediately the hair in the back of my neck stood up. And I was like, there's no way their market's that big. It's been a niche market. Like, there's no way they can be spending that intelligently. And most of the time, in my experience, I don't know that I've ever looked at an AdWords account that within two to three hours, I could find that 50% of the account was waste. That's conservative. And that's not hyperbole. That's not me wanting you to think how smart it is. None of that nonsense. It's just if you follow how to do AdWords, now Google ads based on Google's advice, waste is going to be a big part of that. And that's, it is what it is. So it's, I've never been in a situation, I've been in these many, many, many dozens of times where I've looked at an account and instantly, usually it's in 10 or 15 minutes, you just get through like, yep, here's where all the waste is. So this particular client spending $2.4 million that overall was ROI positive, meaning they were making more money than they were spending at level of 2.5, 2.4 million. It took about somewhere between five and 10 hours, I can't remember total, between five and 10 hours, we reduced their ad spend by $1.7 million and increased their qualified leads by 17% simultaneously. And overwhelmingly, we did that because of the money they were wasting on YouTube, just bleeding money. Every campaign that they had was intellectually sound. Like you could go look at it, you can, you can understand how, how and why somebody created it, you could understand the messaging, you could understand why it should work yet 1.7 and I don't know what percentage of the 1.7 million, but I'm at least a million and a half. And that's not, that's not monopoly money. Like that's not like, oh, they were doing this thing and they wasted a million and a half. But this is $1.5 million that the owner of the company could put in his pocket at the end of the next year and still get better results. It was $1.7 million that could be taken out of their advertising account with no adverse effects, none. In fact, because of the way that the ad spend was working, the things that were actually working were shutting down earlier because they were robbed of ad spend. Their performance increased by when they pulled out, when we pulled out that $1.7 million, they got a much better total result, not like comparatively better results. So just imagine if you have a business spending, and this is sort of a weird thing to do, but imagine you have a business spending $2.5 million, $2.4 million, and you pull out the overwhelming majority of that and get better results and you find out that the reason why you had wasted nearly $2 million was because of this particular platform that was not paying results. You can, I mentioned this so you can sort of understand why I am so hesitant to recommend going down that road. To be clear, YouTube works for lots of people. There's a big move toward YouTube now because it's sort of the wild, wild West. Google has really made themselves, particularly YouTube, an alternative to the nightmares that are experienced on Facebook. I don't know why Google seems to be really lax with their rules on YouTube these days. It's very strange, but they've made that decision somewhere. So there's a big attraction to go, like, give YouTube a try. Oh, it can punish you mercilessly, financially. So that's a very long warning about this. So, yes, there'll be something for YouTube in the traffic engine, but please, please, please proceed with caution. Spend, think about YouTube. This will be my metaphor for YouTube, which I don't know, this is odd, but think about YouTube as your Vegas vacation. Only put money in there that you're willing to lose, right? You sit down at the Blackjack table in Vegas. The odds are slightly against you. They're slightly in the house's favor. When you go in, when you sit down in a casino to play a casino game other than poker, then when you do that, you are putting yourself in a position where over time you are likely to lose. So the money that you bring to the table should be money that you're prepared to lose. When you sit down with, to try YouTube, give it a whirl. Just make sure that the money you're spending on YouTube is Vegas money that you're willing to lose. There's my metaphor. Okay. I'm just reading Noah's comments. Oh, that's good stuff. Oh, I found it. I found a place that uncontested space, cheap traffic, whatever. There are no unexplored niches anymore online. I'm going to Google search. That just aren't. So if there are no advertisers, that generally means there's no traffic. Now, it doesn't mean there's no traffic in your niche. It just means there's no traffic for the search term that you've used to find it. So how would I approach this? I think I would go to... So I think the first thing that I would do is I would go to Amazon and I would find books that... And I'm assuming that I could find books that are representative of something that your audience that you're trying to reach reads. Because what I'm trying to do is I'm trying to find how do people describe what language shows up in the comments. And then when I see that language, I'm going to go back to Google and then just start playing with it. I'm going to look at the book titles. I'm going to look at the book authors. That would be another thing that I would try to do. If there are very popular writers in your niche, I don't always recommend this strategy, but sometimes it works. My belief on paid traffic in general is people tell you what they're looking for with their search intent. So if somebody's looking for Coke, don't try to... In your Pepsi, you don't be like, Hey, if you're looking for Coke, what do you think about Pepsi? And what they think about Pepsi is they're looking for Coke. And that's why they search for Coke. That's not always true. Sometimes, particularly if it's if you're consuming content and you like a certain writer, there's a certain vibe that you like, a podcast that you like, then others that are related can be attractive too, because that's additive.
