My big fear, talking about fear earlier, not to wax too philosophical about fear, but one of my big fears with the example offer is that we were going to succeed and make it look like succeeding was easy. And then, and the reality is, if you're the best marketers in the world, 80% of offers fail. And my fear that's been really causing me angst and I should just get past it, but this is just speaking to all of you as friends and colleagues here, that the real fear has been that we're going to do something that's going to be really successful. And then somebody's going to watch that and they're going to fail and they're going to think it's them. And that bothers me because if somebody goes through the traffic engine and they fail at an offer and they think they've done something wrong and I've inadvertently contributed to that, I would feel horrible because every one of us is going to fail 80% of the time when we do this work. That's the nature of the work. We can stack the deck in our favor lots of ways. We can do the, you know, if you go through module 6.5, the framework, the Facebook testing framework, you can stack the deck lots of ways. But I don't ever want anybody in this program to think that the work that we're doing and the work that you're learning is easy because it's not. And anyway, it sounds like I'm rationalizing and I'm not. It just needs to get done. It's going to get done. I'll shut up about it now. Next question. This guy, Sean, he says a lot of stuff. I'm noticing in the comments, he's maybe saying some dumb shit here. So Sean advised to shut off mobile and tablet devices for Google ads. I'm wondering about those mobile phones because mobile today is 80% of all used devices and therefore Facebook ads targeting just on mobile is a good starting point. That's what I learned from you guys and others. So how come this is different for Google ads and why do you exclude mobile phones for Google ads? I love this question. This is a think about it question, learn to think question. I think this is, I'm going to let Lauren talk about this for Facebook. I might say a couple of things on Facebook, but our behavior on a mobile phone is, yes, this is true demographically, yes, it may be different for your particular market and who your audience is. So understand that there are caveats to all of this. But in general, our behavior on a mobile phone is not analogous to our behavior on an iPad, which is not analogous or another tablet, which is not analogous to our behavior on a laptop or a desktop. Yes, I buy stuff from my phone occasionally. I'm 49, so I'm not a millennial. It's not, it's my primary device. But I don't buy, I don't, I don't interact the same way. And the data that I've seen over time significantly shows that mobile users are by any measure in most businesses of lower, less value than a desktop or a tablet user, not in the existential sense, you know, kumbaya, we're all valuable and wonderful little snowflakes, individual snowflakes, unique in the world. But when you're on your phone, and especially, I think this is especially true for Facebook, when it is for me that the analogy I think about all the time is how many times have I been just scrolling through my phone, waiting in my grocery store to check out? That's not the mindset I want to pay for somebody's attention when they're in. And that's maybe the best way to think about it. You're paying for somebody's attention and all attention is not created equally. My attention when I'm on my phone is not the same as my attention when I'm sitting in front of my desktop, for the most part, broadly speaking. And I think that's true in large audiences. So I'm not saying in the traffic engine, don't do mobile traffic. I'm saying don't do mobile traffic immediately. Go to again, this is stacking the deck in your favor. If we look at a large group of people, and in that large group of people, there is a subset, 20% of them that are on a device that statistically the data shows they are far more likely to actually convert. It's they have a keyboard, it's easy, they can see it, they're not monkeying around with their goofy thumbs, all that stuff. We've stacked the deck in our favor. If we can't convert them in that environment, in the best of all possible situations, how the hell are we ever going to convert them when we add in 80% of them who are looking at their phone driving, who knows, without standing in line at the grocery store? It doesn't make sense to me. Now, your view, when I say you, this is not the person who asked the question, this is you, the broadly speaking you, the curriculum, or the, what is that for anyway? It's you broadly speaking, the Royal you. Your business may be different. You say, no, no, no, no, my business does XYZ and it's all phone and it's an app, then your business, that makes sense for you. But broadly speaking, I want somebody, when I sit down to advertise and I'm saying, I'm going to give you money for your attention. I want really high quality attention. And if I can, if I can architect the system, engineer the system to get better attention, and there are lots of ways to do it, I can do it with mobile, I can do it with geography, I can do it with a time of day, I have lots of levers I can pull, and I want to pull every single one of those to get a result. And that result for me is most likely going to be the gold standard. That's going to be the best. It's not, I can't, for most likely, I can't build my entire business on that, that only that core of traffic. But if I don't know the gold standard, if I don't know what I can get in the best possible scenario, I have nothing to compare what happens when I start adding in other stuff. So let's just say for the sake of argument, I advertise on Google, one campaign, one keyword phrase, and I, and it's for everybody in the United States, doesn't matter where they are, doesn't matter what time of day, and doesn't matter mobile versus non-mobile. And I get a suboptimal result. Maybe I spend $10 and I get $5 back. Well, what's the problem? I have no idea. What do I do? I don't know. Maybe now I have to start monkeying around with something. And I'm like, well, maybe it's time of day. And I change time of day. And then it wasn't time of day. Okay. Well, because, but I don't have a, I don't have a baseline to work from. Now, compare that to if I sit down and I say, I'm going to stack this deck entirely in my favor. I'm going to, it's going to be during business hours, coast to coast, it's going to be in the part of the country where I get whatever it's, I can ship to if I have an actual product, I can ship to in two days. It's for the same cost of regular shipping, because that's how fast UPS gets there. I am going to do desktop only because I believe I'm going to stack the all the deck in there in my favor. And if I do that, and I spend $10 and I get $5 back, guess what? I'm done. It's not going to get better than that. I can't optimize my way out of it. It's clear. The best possible result I'm going to get sucks. Or I spend $10 and I make $40 back. That's my gold standard. Now I add mobile traffic and my $10 goes, or my $40 goes to $20, but I'm still ROI positive. Right? But I know what happened. I know that when I added mobile, I'm still making money, but I'm making less money than that core group, but there's a lot more mobile traffic. So I'm going to make that deal. And then I say, well, maybe my time of day was a little too restrained. So I'm going to expand the time of day. And I expand the time of day by six hours, and my ROI stays the same, but I get a lot more traffic. Excellent. Now I know how time of day impacts my business relative to the gold standard. So I can just keep doing it. I can layer on another campaign that's farther away geographically. I can start doing things. I'm doing them all really intelligently because I know in the best of all possible worlds, the result that I'm going to get, because I've engineered that to give me that baseline. This sounds like something you sort of hear in passing. Yeah, it makes a lot of sense. If I could tell you one thing about how to beat everybody on AdWords, how to think so differently that nobody can compete with you on AdWords, that's part one of two. The other part, this isn't an open loop, I'm like, yeah, sorry, I'll tell you later. The other part is just to cycle faster than they do with your improvements. So once you cycle faster, it says right out of Jonathan, I think, Boyd. That's weird. It's not the, there's a, this is a US, I can't remember why am I, I'm worried that I'm conflating my friend Jonathan Boyd with the other Boyd weird anyway. Sorry, I didn't mean to go on that weird tangent. I just hadn't ever made that connection. But this was it Jonathan Schubert. Now I'm thinking of the, so 60 second Boyd, I have his, okay, so now I have to explain it because I've done this really weird thing. So it's a John Boyd. Yeah, yeah. So John Boyd was a US, he's an aviator, a pilot. But they called him 60 second Boyd. And the reason he got this name is he had 267 air engagements, training engagements, and that he would make him a bet that you could start on his six o'clock position, the most vulnerable position for a fighter pilot, where the enemy is right behind him, six o'clock, if you imagine a clock face, and that within 30 seconds he would swap and he would be in your six o'clock position. And he never lost an air engagement. And he is the founder of the Uda Loop, this an easy, we all go through this four part process, we observe the situation, and we orient to it, we make a decision and we act. And whoever can go through that cycle faster, we said you're getting inside your, your opponent's decision cycle. So not to glorify war in any way or glorify anything associated with war in any way, but the US strategy in the first Gulf War, that was so overwhelmingly successful from a military perspective, not to diminish the humanitarian disaster in any way, shape, or form, but from a purely military perspective that was entirely informed on Boyd's theories of maneuver warfare. He was a brilliant strategic mind. So with that as background, early in my career in, in AdWords, I read Boyd's work. For some reason, I mapped that to AdWords. So the two parts, if you want to be the best in the world, Google ads, the two things you need to be able to do better than anybody else are narrow the field down to a pristine core that is most likely, you've stacked the deck entirely in your favor using levers like geography, time of day, and mobile versus non-mobile. You get that gold standard before you add other things. And simultaneously with that, you go through the Uda Loop faster than everybody else. You get some data, you observe it, you orient yourself to it. That's the critical part that most people miss. You look at the days and what does this mean? And you might need, in your Uda Loop might require three days, depending on your ad budget, seven days. That's one of the reasons why I recommend fewer campaigns. So you can put more money into fewer campaigns. So you get data faster. All of this goes into this thinking. This is the underlying architecture of the traffic engine. This is how you do bad words better than everybody else. And what happens is you have fewer things that you're doing that are so narrowly focused that you cycle faster than everybody else does. You make your improvements faster than everybody else does. And when you do that, at some point, the math does not ever allow the competition to catch up. When you are making, in very simple terms, if you make an improvement, a minor improvement, basic A, B testing every week, twice a week, and your competition makes it every week, within two or three months, you are so far ahead of them in improvements that they can never catch up. The math just isn't there unless they dump huge amounts of money in assuming the market supports them. So this is a really weird tangent about a very simple question. But I want you to understand the thinking behind this. And that thinking is you have to engineer the best possible circumstance for your business to get the best possible result. Even if that result is small, that data is critically important. Now you know what is likely to happen under the best case scenario, so that when you make this scenario less than optimal, you know the impact of those decisions and you can choose them at a granular level wisely. So if you go from no mobile to all mobile and things fall apart, you might go from, you might go back and say, okay, let's go back to no mobile, but then I'll add mobile in, but I want to, I'm only willing to pay half of what I'm paying, you can just modify or add words, half of what I pay for desktop, I'll pay for mobile, because it's just not that much. And then you find, and then you're like, okay, that kind of works. What if I do 40% that doesn't work as well, we're about 45%. Ooh, there's the sweet spot. And you're always doing that, you're always making those decisions because you know what worked. It's a really long answer. Holy smokes. From your perspective, Lauren, totally different. How does this map to Facebook, what do you think about the same question, turning on, turning off mobile traffic on Facebook, knowing that mobile is such an enormous part of the Facebook traffic? Yeah, generally, mobile is going to get about 80% of your traffic. And I'm sure as time goes on, that may creep up to 85, 90%. So it's a huge, huge, huge part of your Facebook and Instagram traffic, because people use the platform differently. When you're waiting in line, when you have a spare moment in the day for whatever reason, people open up Facebook, they scroll. I generally just go with include mobile, because that's what most of the people are going to view it through anyway. There are occasions where I've, I don't think I've ever set it to desktop only, at least not recently. You're better off just kind of letting Facebook, the Facebook algorithm do it. I've heard of some people optimizing, they'll have like one set of creative optimized for desktop and one set of creative optimized for mobile and running those both in tandem. That's not really a best practice anymore. What I have done is, if I was going to a webinar where someone was going to register for a webinar, particularly if it's like an on-demand webinar, where then they're going to sit and they're going to watch 50 minute, you know, an hour long video, I have had that before where you can actually set it up in Facebook where you, you can target mobile, but only if they're connected to Wi-Fi. So the theory would be obviously if they're out roaming around on their data, they're less likely to watch an hour long webinar. So there have been an occasion where I said, all right, I'm going to keep mobile in there, but I'm going to set it up only for Wi-Fi. And that worked. It actually worked really well. Interestingly enough, it didn't work any better or worse. I tested it later, opening up all mobile and it didn't work any better or worse. Then again, this was only a couple of months ago when here in the US, we were on various stages of lockdown and across the different states. So that may be part of the reason why it worked then. Something to try out, but generally it's not, I wouldn't classify that as low hanging fruit for results. Generally, that's just something if you, you had a compelling reason to test it. Otherwise, just open it up and let Facebook figure it out. I almost skipped over the rest of these questions accidentally. I'm glad I didn't. There's some good stuff in here. All right. Fourth question in this list. What's the best way to use a multi-page pre-sell site with a Google ad? I've built a six-page multi-page pre-sell site, but it feels long for a Google ad where a target group has a more urgent need to solve a problem or fill their need or desire. How can I approach this? So this is a very important question. Matching the intent of your audience to what happens next, very important. So there is no standard answer. Like six pages is too long, but 4.5 is perfect. Like there is no answer. The answer is what is required to establish the beliefs that lead your prospect from the first thing that they did to the next thing that you want them to do. That's really the way to think about this. So if somebody, in the example I use all the time in the traffic engine is the leaky roof example just because we can all imagine what that would be like. If somebody searches Google for fix a leaky roof or roofing contractor near me or whatever, I'm going to go out on a limb here. Andre may disagree with me. This would be fun. I'm going to go out on a limb here and say that you do not need a six-page multi-page presale site that leads to a PDF download of what to look for in a roofing contractor and then a five-part soap opera sequence before that leads to your phone number. I'm guessing what they want to know is are you available right now and do you have 24-hour service and how long is it going to take to get somebody over to my house because my roof is leaking? That's an extreme example. Perry Marshall is famous for using the phrase he uses. Does it the prospect have a bleeding neck? That's kind of gross, sure, but we get it. If you've got a problem where you've got a bleeding neck, you're not going to do anything else until you solve this problem. That doesn't mean the multi-page presale site is helpful because maybe you have a problem that there is a standard solution for and that standard solution or conventional wisdom isn't ideal and there are problems that that person doesn't know about. Your expertise is saying, listen, I know you're looking for X in the way most people solve for X is this, this, and this and we get it, but here's the problem. When you do that, there's this other thing that's going to happen and we don't want that to happen. Here's why. We do this instead. Maybe that's two pages. Maybe it's one long page. The mechanics don't really matter, but what matters is what is required for the prospect to believe that you are the right solution to their problem, that you are the right, it doesn't have to be problem solution, but you're that you're the right decision for them to make. Whatever is required for that is what you need to show them. Now, in some situations, you know, like I can, I'm imagining the roofing situation. I've had this happen, so it's very visceral for me. So I have, I remember it was the December 23rd. I was supposed to go with my family to Texas the next day. My wife is from Texas. I came downstairs and there was an enormous puddle of water on my floor where there was not supposed to be an enormous puddle of water and I looked up, I live in a renovated barn. I looked up and the ceiling was wet. I, I, at that point, here's my only question. Who can I call? Who can be here to fix that problem the fastest? And the person who gets here the fastest I'm going to pay. And in that, in that particular example, if somebody, if I call somebody, they said, well, we could be there tomorrow and it'll cost $1,000 or we can be there in 20 minutes and it'll cost $2,000. I would have been willing to pay the $2,000. It was that important. That's not every situation. So you have to match, and this is where I think there's some granularity that you need to think about. If you have a, let's just assume for this conversation that you have 10 keyword phrases, and it's a spectrum, a temperature spectrum. Glenn Livingston came up with the term bull's eye keyword. I borrowed that from now. I love that. There might be a search where that person, they're looking exactly for what you have to offer. And they already believe that the intent in their search is very clear. And it's a tiny part of your market. Maybe for every hundred clicks, it's five. Those people, you might make the decision to send them to an abbreviated entry point in your business because you can tell by their search intent that they, they already believe all the things that you need them to believe. They're looking for what you have very specifically, that they're looking for the thing. So you might decide that maybe it's a thousand words on a page to confirm that that thing that they're very specifically looking for is the thing that you offer. But then as you get farther away from that bull's eye, you might have people who are searching for things that are less specific because maybe they're more in a research phase, or maybe they're, they're learning more, or they're looking at lots, lots of options in there. And that's expressed in their search intent. Well, at that point, then they, there's, there's more to talk with them about. There's more to explain that then leads to that last page. So you could, and you have to think out loud structurally, you could build a multi-page pre-sell site almost in reverse and then have, and I wouldn't do it this way to be clear. This is a conceptual idea. But then you're driving the specificity of the traffic is sent to deeper pages within that is one way to think about it. Don't do it that way to be clear. Just think about it that way. Like, what does somebody who makes this search need to know before they know that I am the right person, or my product is the right product, and give that to them? And however long that is, is the right answer. Now come back and edit with a heavy hand and make sure that you're not repeating yourself and then window it down. I think six pages in general is probably, that's a little tough. And I don't know if these are six pages, like 250 words of pages and who knows. And it's more of like a staccato pop, pop, pop, pop through the six pages, or if it's 6,000 to 1500 word pages, that's a, that's a legit effort on your client's part. But it may be awesome. Again, I don't want to suggest, like, I love putting barriers in front of the wrong people. But that's, that's the, that's the business I'm in, right? Like most of the people who do what I do or do what I've done are in the business of removing barriers to get more people of lower quality to the end to make a number go up that's meaningless. And I'm in the opposite business. I'm in the business of putting every conceivable hurdle in front of the wrong people to get them to drop out of the race so that only the people who remain are the people who should be there. That's my game. Yeah, you, you can play either game, you can play a variation of those games as closer. But I like getting in the way of people who are not the right prospects. And I like to think about how to do that. So your six page multi-page presale site might be the best way to do that possible. You know, I just think about just constantly, it's the reverse of the traditional sales. You know, Matthew, you say, well, I'm not really sure I like it in black. Well, we've got one in white. I'm the opposite. They're like, well, I'm not sure, you know, I like the car in black. I'm like, fine, maybe this isn't the right car for you. And it's like, I maybe go too far in that direction. But I like this idea of putting lots of barriers up, even for a Google ad, right? It just all comes down to like, for your business, what is the thing that creates the path for you, for the people who are going to be good prospects to follow? How do you show them the way? How do you pull them forward? And if you can pull a prospect forward who has through the, what they've shown in their search intent, if you can pull them in 1500 words from, okay, this is the right place to be. This person, they have credibility, they have authority, they have empathy, they get me, they've got a really good solution. And I want to do what's next. Can you do that 1500 words for a subset of your audience? Do it. And if there's another subset of your audience that needs 3000 words, 4000 words, because you need to establish more of the foundation earlier, then do that. Don't treat them all the same. I think it's part of the message here. So have a lot of fun with that. I mean, it's not a, there is no, again, this is a learn to think course. So I want you to think deeply about this. Like, this is what somebody searched for. What do I think they need to get to make a really good decision to become a customer of mine? And in what ways would I prevent the wrong people from making that journey? Those are two questions to ask yourself. And then you just build a mousetrap to do that. They put stuff in the way of the wrong people. And then in an example of this, say you have a 45 minute webinar, and you sell at the end, and then you go through a pretty typical webinar, you cover, you tell your story, you go through three main points, and each of those three points has three points underneath that. And it leads to you. All roads lead to Rome again. And you get to this point where the offer is made. And it's the logical conclusion of everything that you've talked about. Conventional wisdom says, get as many people to opt into that as possible. And what I start looking for is where can I throw up roadblocks in the first roadblock you can throw up? Is he, by the way, you have to, this is a webinar, and you have to give your email address for it. A lot of people are like, Nope, I'm not going to do that. Cool. See it. Like, I'm good. Like, I'm glad I told you, I'm glad I didn't waste your time. You didn't waste mine. And oh, by the way, it's 45 minutes. I don't watch 45 minute webinar. Great. Move along. Right. Like, those are the hurdles we're throwing up. It sounds like I'm trying to be a jerk. I'm not. I'm just trying to be kind to everybody. And I'm trying to say upfront, and anything that I'm doing, here is what is required to be a customer of mine. I believe I have value for the right people. I don't want to waste time of the wrong people. And if, and here's how to know if you're not the right people, so that I can respect your attention, you can go do something that's more in line with your interest. And it's the highest form of respect. It sounds like I'm being a jerk. And it's not that at all. It is the utmost respect for the other person to be able to say in advance, here are five ways to know if you shouldn't go past, go in this situation. It's not quite that obvious. But that's what I'm thinking in the back of my mind. What could I tell you that would make you turn around if you're the wrong person? And that's the right thing to do for you. And parenthetically, that's the right thing to do for me, because I don't want to have to waste my time on it either. So it sounds like I'm being a jerk, but I don't mean it that way. All right, that was Google specific. So we'll just jump to the next thing. Can you give some advice what to write in the Facebook ad headlines and subtitle? There's nothing mentioned about that only about the ad copy itself. There's nothing shared about that. And I think that we have an impact on CTRL, pre-qualifying. This is a Lauren question, but I can't miss the opportunity to run my mouth about it for a second. So here's what I hate about thinking in headlines. It is really easy to craft a headline that grabs somebody's attention to get them to, especially curiosity. And there's a lot talking traditional copywriting and others on how to grab somebody with that. It's usually something that's intellectually interesting and emotionally compelling. And there's a copywriter, I think, called these fascinations. And so many copywriting courses are built around this idea of fascinations. And I just, for whatever reason, that bothers the hell out of me. I get it. And Lauren can probably make a really good case for this. So this is one person's perspective. So take it in that context. I don't need to fascinate you. I want your attention. I do want to attract your attention with something, but I don't want to put myself in a position where I'm too focused on grabbing your attention in a way that's going to give me a false signal. So I think probably I go too far in the direction of just like the Volvo philosophy of selling, which is, you know, it's boxy, but it's safe, right? Like I go too far in that direction. Like, here's the thing. This is what it does. If this is you, you probably want it. If this is not you, you probably don't. Like that to me is respectful marketing, but there's a lot of, there's a lot of nuance there. So I offer that as context to say, don't try to make your headline so compelling that it grabs the wrong attention. Let it get the right attention. And now for a much better answer to this question. Lauren, how would you answer this question? Well, I think what he's meaning by subtitle might be the news feed description. I generally don't spend too much time there at all, just kind of about the author or just a brief description of the product or something because that only appears of a small minority of the time and no one pays attention to it. So, you know, put something there. Otherwise it'll just default to whatever wordage is on your landing page, but don't stress about it. In terms of the headline, generally what I found works best is kind of reiterating the main selling points of what you're hitting in your introduction. So in your intro, the first two or three lines that someone sees on mobile before the Seymour link there, whatever pain points you're hitting, whatever benefits you're presenting, the name of the product, reiterating those in the headline just seems to work well surprisingly. Another thing is I like to use the headline to kind of show what the end result is. Is this a free ebook? Is this a webinar? Is this a $27 product? Is this a blog post? So sometimes I may just put like, you know, bracket, master class, you know, bracket, and then kind of you can fill in the rest of the headline. It is a secondary way just to kind of compliment what you're already hitting in your introduction. And you can try a few different ones if you want to. Generally, I think that my philosophy on the headline is just do a good one that doesn't mess things up. And generally your image, your video, and then your introduction is going to do 95% of the heavy lifting. I knew you'd give a better answer. That's way better. All right. I lied earlier. By definition, they want quick results and they want to sell ASAP, nodding my head up and down, as I know, I know Lauren is too. I really do not want to be on social media all day at the same time. I'm not an SEO guy. Sean, how do you use sphere of influence principles to grow a new agency? Or how would you use sphere of influence principles to grow a new agency if you had to start again today and had 12 months to make it work? So I, in parentheses, I don't need clients immediately. Okay, really good stuff here. But I want to do is I want to answer Kevin's questions, but it's simultaneously that are more specific to an agency business, but then simultaneously, I want to pair that with a corollary thinking about how it applies beyond just working with clients. The first question is a, I've seen this more times than I can count. It is very near and dear to me and it's so pervasive. And that the question really is, when you're, when you're working with clients and in parallel with that, when you're trying to get something going for yourself, there are these two in Andre call these two opposing forces. That's a really brilliant insight from Andre that you have in the one hand, I need something to happen now, like the cash register needs to ring. But on the other hand, the actions that I take to get the cash register, cash register to ring faster are most likely taking me farther away from what I actually want, which is long-term happy repeat customers. So that's, and if you have sphere of influence, there's a rich chef in, there's a video of rich chef in there. And he says what I think is one of the most profound things that's the way to think about marketing ever spoke and riches is just a brilliant individual. But the way he describes how he's been successful, he makes it a formula. And the formula is this, what you think is the problem is X. So you do Y. But the real problem is Z. And why makes Z worse? How many of you are going, what, like, does he have a head injury? I don't have a head injury. Let's think about this. Let's put some, let's fill in the blanks. What you think is the problem is you're not exercising enough. So you join CrossFit and kill yourself in the gym. And then when you do that, your cortisol goes up, super stressed, you're hungry, you eat a ton, and you actually gain weight. What you think is the problem is you're not exercising enough. So you exercise yourself crazy, but exercising actually makes the problem worse. And gets you farther away from result. That's the formula. So that formula, that frame of formula, from those wrong word framework, that framework, we can overlay here. You know, what, and if we think about it, you know, what, what we think is the problem is we need customers right now. So to get customers right now, you know, we do all these, you fill in the blank, we do all these crazy things, you know, we give away a million bonuses, we put a countdown timer on, we have false urgency, we, you know, bye, bye, bye, bye. But the reality is when we do all of that, we actually get far fewer customers. That's the truth. That's this is not conjecture. This is not hyperbole. This is between Andre and I, we have over four decades of experience. We, this is a fundamental truth that we have uncovered. We see it everywhere. We talk about it all the time, that when you optimize for these short term signals, you do that at the expense of the long game. That however is, there's not a lot of solace in that. You're like, great, I get it, but I, I still need to make money right now, right? That, and from a client perspective, you really have, so this is the question, I'll pull the question out, but the first part that Kevin has, how did you or do you balance the direct conflict of thinking when you own an agency? So the short answer to that is not well, meaning that I have become increasingly, you know, curmudgeonly in dealing with clients. And I have, and of course, and recently fired clients because I don't do client work anymore. But before that, it was an enormous source of frustration for me. So there's a gentleman interviewed by Tim Ferriss named Graham Duncan, a talent expert, and highly recommend that interview. It's phenomenal. But one of the things Graham Duncan mentioned in that interview, he said, our genius is right next to our dysfunction. I don't want to be cautious about thinking about my genius, because I say it with all humility. But if I do have something that rises to the level for me of genius, is it is my ability to see with clarity how to get from A to B. Like, I really can see that. And it makes sense to me, and I can connect the dots at the larger scale. So I can see like, okay, this is what really matters. That's my genius. Adjacent to that, right next to that is my dysfunction, which is if somebody, if I have that conversation and somebody agrees that the data is accurate, the assessment is accurate, they agree with everything, but they're like, yeah, but I still want to do it this other way. I can't do that. And when I say I can't, I don't mean, you know, pay me enough money, and I'll do it. I mean, my brain doesn't work that way. We just, Lauren and I just had this conversation the other day, I want to be very cautious not to do anything that would point to what it was. But this was the gist of the conversation. This was the gist of the conversation. We're selling our core offer on Facebook. And we're about it break even and lots of things are going on. This is a market dramatically affected by COVID. So lots of things are going on. So number one, we're trying to do this thing that we're starting to have some success with. But we want to try something different. Instead of selling our core offer, which the price point is in the thousands of dollars, what we're going to do instead is we want to sell a really low cost version in the tens of dollars. Okay, right, that makes sense. The way I would look at that, say, okay, so if you're selling a low cost offer, it's because you want to you want to get to the high cost offer. So the question was, in the the length of time this business has been in business, how many people go like ascend through that path, they buy the really low cost offer and they eventually buy the core offer. And the answer was the data show that nobody does that. So now here's the data that I have in mind, and then follow up by what we want to try it. So this is my genius says, Hey, what's the thing that we're trying to do is sell the core offer, let's engineer a system that sells the core offer, here are the steps, let's do it, let's figure it out. My dysfunction shows up when the client says the following, we want to do X in the hope that it leads to Y. But in the history of our business, X has never led to Y. And we've been in business a long time. My brain short circuits, like, because to me, it sounds like we want to try this thing that we know doesn't work. And in some people, that's fine, whatever, you're the client. And that's sometimes the answer, right? And that's this really, this is the honest answer to your question. And this is really where I got back to where I've been most successful dealing with this question. And I think this is the answer to your, how do you balance this? There are a couple of ways. One, and this is what contributes to your second question to one, I think you need to create a manifesto and put a stake in the ground to attract the people who you want to work with, who are looking for you, there are a lot fewer of them, but there are also a lot fewer people trying to reach them. And I think you want to do it with a little bit of attitude. And you don't have to be a jerk. But I think you need to point out the problem in very clearly and say, and I wouldn't say this is how my clients have done it, you can say, this is what I have observed. People want this thing to be true. Yet everything they do is undermining the thing that they really want. And I won't do it that way. And then you show them how you very like, this is this is a multi multi page pre cell site, show them show pictures show describe it, put it right in their face and say, if this is you, and what you're what you really want is you have an offer that you want to sell that you want to scale you want to do all of these things. Yet you are you're undermining every effort to do that because you're doing these five dumb things. I mean, you decide how much animation you want to add to that I would do it with a lot of attitude just because that matches my personality. I think that's the front end. And recognize that 99% of the people who go to this are like, screw this guy. It's good, right? Because then you have to work with them. Right? That's one path that you can take. There's another path that you can take. And both of these are equally valid. The other path that you can take is and I did this a few times, it's oddly liberating right up until it's not like for me, it was oddly liberating at first, but the time bomb was ticking and I finally imploded. So what I what I did, that it was just it was such an obvious realization is I realized at one point, my job is not to decide for the client. This is quite a long time ago. My job is not to define success for the client. My job is to hear the client's definition of success and determine if I am able to provide that for them. And I mentioned this in some of the client services content. I need to define that broadly across psychological factors too. Like how do they want to feel about the work? Like this client I was mentioning before, they're what makes them feel good is to feel like they're doing something. It's empowering. We're doing something. Maybe that something's not going to lead to a perfect result, but we're doing something. So one approach that I could take is I could I could rise to that and say, okay, you're hiring me to accomplish this thing that you have said is important to you. And I have been an honest, high integrity actor and said to you, here are my concerns. I think what you want is this thing. And I think that the way we're going about it is not going to get you that thing. However, I also recognize that you are the client and it is my responsibility to provide you with what you want. So I'm willing to do that. That sounds really great. We can all hold hands. We can sing kumbaya. We can feel really good about it. But let me tell you the other side of that that I learned the hard way. When the thing that the client wanted to do fails spectacularly, and you knew it was going to fail, and you told them it was going to fail, and you showed them precisely how it was going to fail. And then it fails, there is going to be zero attention paid to the fact that you said it was going to fail. That is the most common form of client amnesia I have ever seen in my entire life, where the client has a dumbfounded look on their face. What? You didn't think this was going to work? Why didn't you tell me? I don't know. Here are the 45,000 words of emails I wrote saying we shouldn't do this. I know someone who has done a lot of client services work. And one of the phrases he said, no one knows this person, too. People hire consultants so they have somebody to blame when things go wrong. People hire agencies for that, too. It is comical. How you craft your business, and I get a sense from Kevin from the questions you have sent in different contexts, and these questions and the depth of thought you are putting into it, that is not going to be you. You are not going to be comfortable with that. I think for you, the answer is put your stake in the ground and don't be subtle about it. Build the front end and say, this is who I am. If this isn't who you want to work with, cool. There are lots of people who do this differently. I did this for years. I had the worst website for the longest time as a design build company, which was hysterical. Here is what we do. Here are my five competitors who also do it. You should go look at them, too. And then come back. Go talk to them and then come back. I had more people comment on that. We didn't even call your competitors. Who does that? To me, it was like, you know what? The thinking, and I tend to externalize this, and again, we are all seeing like, I am a psychologist couching with my dysfunction. I am highly motivated by righteous indignation. That was my early righteous indignation. If you call me and say, we are interested in this project or whatever, but we are also going to be talking to your competitors. That dynamic, screw you, don't call me back. I didn't say it. That is what I was thinking. I engineered that into my sales process. Here is what we do. Here is our experience. Here is how long I have done it. Here is who we have done it for. Here are other people who do what I do. Go talk to them first. Go find them. And then feel free. Reach out at that point. And what I was doing is I was taking away that ability to say, we are talking to your competitors, too. Because, good, I already told you to do that. We are on the same page. It sounds crazy. And it matches my personality. And I don't recommend this for everybody. But I think for you, ultra high integrity, you are doing the work, you are going deep in the material, you really want to learn this stuff. I would put that stake right in the ground. This is who I am. This is how I do things. This is why I do things. 100% transparent. Here is how I think about it. Here is why I know this is the right approach. This is in order to work with me, you need to agree that this is how we are going to do it. And if you can't, that is perfectly okay. There are lots of people who will work with you to get you suboptimal results. I would even say it like that. I would just put it right out there. There are plenty of people who are willing to do it a different way. But I won't do it that way. So when you work with me, you are agreeing in advance that we are going to do the right thing to build your business the right way. Period. Like that, wow. I am excited. I am just thinking about you doing this. So do that. Now, let's take these questions and let's apply them to all of us who are not in the agency setting. What is the lesson that we can learn from this? Lesson number one is, and this is the bedrock core of everything that Andre and I talk about all the time, do not optimize for things that are not the thing that is most important for your business. The thing that is most important for your business is to create happy customers. Now, what is a happy customer? Noah and I had this conversation earlier this week. Just full disclosure, Noah and I are friends. So when I am referencing Noah, just know that in context. We are having this conversation back and forth. What is a happy customer? Why is that the best metric? We have really deep in this conversation. It is a hard metric because we can make an objective thing that approximates it like you could call true customers. So a true customer might be a customer who buys and does not refund. That is not a happy customer. We are a happy customer. Do they reply? Are they a super fan? Now there is lots of nuance. But we all kind of know. We would see it if we saw how we know. So from that context, like, don't, taking this question that, from the agency question that Kevin asked to all of us when we are thinking about non-agency environment, it all boils down to this phrase that I apologize for swearing about this. Don't do dumb shit. That is really the name of the game we are in. And one of the dumbest things we can do is try to get a result so fast that we undermine the result that we actually care about. Everything is in service to the result that we actually care about. Now, the caveat to that is if you are doing something and you need to produce sales to make that something exist, maybe you have gone out on your own. Maybe your job disappeared because of COVID. I don't know your situation. But if something happens, then that is the context in which you are operating. And then do the thing that sells faster. Because within a large audience, there is a subset of that audience. I am one of these people that I will buy something in 30 seconds after I am made aware of it if the promise is compelling. It matches up with the need that I have and the price point is right. I did this yesterday. It was like $60, $67. I bought it if it is miserable and it is off. I could refund, I suppose. But if it is not great, it is not great. It is not a $7,000 thing. Some people do that. Reach those people first and be upfront about it. Here is what I am doing. This is going to be what is involved. But right now what I am really looking for are the people who need this most. And I want to get this in their hands as fast as possible. But no one exchange for that. It is going to be a little rough around the edges. And I am just trying as fast as possible to get this thing that I have been spending some time thinking about or working, whatever it is. I am getting it out as fast as possible. It is going to look like this. It is not going to be super polished. But if this is something you need right now, that is great and maybe you are going to get it for less. You are going to be a founding member, whatever it is. Sell the hell out of that to the subset of the market that does not care about that other stuff. And there are plenty of them. Do that. Just do not get into this business of after you have done that, saying, well, that worked really well. Let us just keep doing that. No. Go for the rest of the audience too. The rest of the audience is a longer cycle, probably. So I want to be paying attention to the time here too. So I want to be cautious. I just love these questions. Which is funny because I thought there were agency questions and then when I actually spent the time to read them and go deep, these are just broadly applicable. So the second question that he asked about, so how would I use the sphere of influence principles that grew and outgrow a new agency if I had to start again, I would do it again, be cautious how I would do it. I personally would do it the way I described for you. But be very cautious that I am bringing 22 years of expo all to 27 years because I did the work before I owned an agency. I am bringing 27 years of experience to the party and I am bringing a certain reputation to the party and the context really matters. So I can pull off a variation of this where if I could put my stake in the ground and say, this is who I am, this is what I have done, this is how I work, if this does not resonate with you, that is awesome. We are still friends, but move along. That is what I would do. If I had to do, if I change your question slightly, slightly, if I wanted to grow an agency where I did not have any of that benefit, what would I do instead? I would actually go back to the audience and offer master class, which incidentally, that was not written prescriptively as a course. It was written descriptively of how I actually do that. So what you are seeing in the audience and offer class is me writing how that works, how it has worked. I have done it a bunch of times and we are going to update that to combine some things. So it is a description of what I actually do. I would go back to that. I would go through that. What I would be looking for is I want to position myself very differently so that I am a category of one. I would look across what other agencies were doing, how they were describing their things. I would talk to people, I would call people, and I would be very clear. This is what I am thinking about doing. I am not trying to work with you. I do not have the intention to work with you, but here is one of the most curious about, and when you listen to the recording of Jonathan Boyd, you will get a lot of insights on this. I would do that. Then I would make a value proposition that to me was the thing that everybody was asking for. What did they want most? I do not think it would be many things. I think it would be one thing. Category of one, we do this one thing that is really important better than anybody else. Here are all the things we do not do. That is how I would approach it. Here are the 50 things that an agency could do. These are the 50 things that your agency might do. Here are the 49 of them that we do not do. We believe this is the most important one. Here is why we believe it is the most important one. I have a feeling that clients would want to get results very quickly. I would connect with their deepest desire. I would present it to them. I would project to them, if this is you, if this is the thing that matters most to you, if you want to work with an agency that is 100% focused on getting this particular result, this particular way, in this particular timeframe, then that is what we do and that is all that we do. I would be up front. If you want SEO and you want it, great. We can recommend people to do that. We do not do that. We do this one thing. It is just so that when you are thinking in your mind, it happens that one thing we do is the thing that you want more than anything else. That is how I would position it. Then the sphere of influence principles come in because what I am really doing here is I am pulling them toward me by saying, here is the thing you want more than anything else. This is the thing that wakes you up in the middle of the night. If it is the one thing, if the genie in the bottle popped up, it is the one wish you want granted. I am the genie in the bottle and I can make it happen. I would take them through that entire journey so that by the end of it, all they want to do is whatever is next. I would not persuade for a moment. I would pull them toward me. I would crank the desire dial up to 11. Again, only because I am also willing to do the work to be able to deliver on the promise. This is not sales bullshit. This is not me making up a big story and then trying to figure out how I am going to deliver on it. I am going to be able to deliver the goods. That is one of the reasons I want to focus on narrowly. If I focus narrowly, I can be the best in the world at something pretty fast if I remove all the distractions. I am willing to do that work, but I am only going to do that in service to something I know is ultra important to a client. I will not do any of that. The other thing I would do that would be a little different than what I have done in the past. I would not change what I have done. It would be exactly what I wanted. The thing I would change is I would do that one thing at the expense of everything else and I would almost be indignantly angry if somebody asked me to do something else. We are like, hey, we love what you are doing here. Could you just stop talking? No. We do this. That would be for my own benefit. I know that sounds weird. Anyway, I want to be respectful of time. We usually try to cap this at three hours and we are getting close. Great questions, everybody. I want to go to the Q&A. There are questions, but I am not sure. I can probably bang those out pretty quickly. Many of them. Some of them are related to previous questions and I have had a chance to read them while you were. What don't you answer the first one about ads manager? Sure. Actually, let me see. Yeah, this one. They are kind of related. We were talking earlier about someone who said their audience was primarily in Instagram. They say they had a follow-up here in the Q&A that when they go to automatic placement, about 80% of their impressions go to Instagram. Does that mean that Facebook is telling them to focus on Instagram versus Facebook? If yes, should I keep on setting up Instagram ads from Facebook manager or from the Instagram app? That is interesting that 80% of the impressions are going to Instagram. I would not necessarily phrase it. That is Facebook telling you to focus on that as in you need to exclude Facebook, but just keep that in mind. I would still do it within the Facebook ads platform within the same way that you place a Facebook ad as opposed to through the Instagram app. Facebook just treats Instagram as a separate placement, not really a different platform. Still within the Facebook ads platform, I would still leave it on automatic placement. The only thing I would really change is when you are coming up with your creative. Keep in mind that 80% of the people are seeing it on Instagram, so maybe have the creative optimized for Instagram viewers, kind of like we talked about earlier, really prioritizing that image of video that doesn't go over two minutes if you do use videos and then the copy that is shorter and then the first line, you will see fewer characters. That should hopefully take care of that question also later on. He said, he would appreciate it if I could recommend some Instagram ads courses that I trust. I don't really know of anyone that is totally amazing. A lot of Instagram education is going to come in the context of Facebook ads because it is really just a flavor of Facebook advertising. I cut my teeth on digital marketer and I would say they are trustworthy. They are very beginner focused, but that might be a place you could look for for Instagram specific stuff. Other than that, anyone that teaches about Facebook is probably going to touch on Instagram, particularly a lot of the e-com. I don't know your particular industry if you are focused on e-com versus selling information products versus high ticket lead generation and maybe different. A lot of the e-com advertisers, Instagram is major there, so there is a lot of education based around e-com that may be helpful. He said also custom conversion follow-up question. I don't currently have 50 to 70 sales per week, but I have 3,000 customers accumulated over two years. Can I use that email list to set up my custom conversion for sales versus leads? Or does it have to be a weekly sales number? No, you can use that list to set up like a custom audience that you can then make a look-alike off of, but it wouldn't help with your custom conversion. The custom conversion is, well, I guess, yeah, I wouldn't do it. It would be more complicated, but just set up the custom conversion and get that regular input. Facebook does need a regular influx of those conversions to kind of keep it dialed in and going well. Another, Kevin had a question. He said, I understand warming up the audience is in line with creating better prospects. I think that was us talking about when we were talking about the videos and branding campaigns. Sean, do you want to chime in or you just want me to keep going? Yeah, and I think it's, I'll just chime in very quickly. So, yes, is the short answer. It is in line, but it's also light attention. I think that's the only distinction that I would make. So, I think you can actually make an entire sphere of influence informed narrative arc without a multi-page pre-sale site. I think the genius of sphere of influence is that Andre taught a method that actually instilled principles. And when you learn the method, you also learn the principles. So, from the micro, he taught the macro, which I think was brilliant. And from that perspective, I think you could daisy chain pieces of, you could have like engagement pieces with video and with other components to start the beginning of the multi-page, like would effectively be a multi-page pre-sale site from a content perspective. So, I don't get locked into the view of the big insight for me. I was with the first people, the first beta users for sphere of influence. And then there's an aha moment where I was like, well, this doesn't just have to be on the page. It could be at a system level. It could be these touch points that could be establishing things like credibility and authority. So, I think about it much more broadly. I think, yes, it's in line with what you're describing, but at the same time, I think you can go broader than that. So, yeah, run with it. Yeah. And also too, you got to think in terms of your resources. Yeah, absolutely. Warming up an audience is going to give you better prospects. And if you bought every single Super Bowl commercial this year and bought full page articles in the New York Times every single edition, you would definitely have better prospects and you'd have warmer audiences, but that's just not cost effective. So, a lot of times it is when you can have a long form Facebook ad, for instance, that is the sole touch point that takes them through that journey, it's doing that in the most cost effective way. But that being said, try it a different way, see if it works. Another question, Sean here, the guy we were talking earlier about, he said that the reverse of that strategy, what if you went for conversions and then if people land on the buying page but they don't buy, you retarget them with videos to reengage or indoctrinate them and then push them down a path of education that ends up with the offer at a different angle. But would you just retarget making the offer again if they don't buy the first time? Yeah, this is a great question. So, a couple of ways to think about this. First question you have to answer is why didn't somebody buy? And this is the question nobody answers. So, whatever you get, 100 people see your offer page and of those 100 people, two of them become customers. 98 didn't. That's pretty strong signal that your offering is not compelling or what you did before the offer is not very compelling. And then 98 people would go, if I only get two more of those through retargeting, I double my conversion, sure. But start by doing a thought exercise. Here's what everybody did before they got here, before they saw this page. And then here's what they did when they got here. And then go look at your Google Analytics, whatever it is, 100 of them, for every 100 you saw this page, two purchased. 50%, likely, your data was your data, 50% or more were not on that page longer than maybe five seconds. So, what does that even mean? Is it even real traffic? Who knows? But what was that? Half the people didn't even engage. Why were they here? What happened? And then the remainder, what does that remainder of traffic look like? What was going on there? Like, you think about the textual differences. Let's say that of the, let's say, some significant portion of your traffic gets to your order page and they stay for 10 minutes and they don't buy. That's really weird, right? That's, that's really like, you've, you've engaged them for 10 minutes, yet they still didn't buy. What the hell happened versus you engaged them for 20 seconds and they didn't buy? Well, that, those are two very different strategies to figure out what went wrong. And all of the solutions start with knowing the problem. And this sounds so obvious, but I can't tell you how many times I've been in situations where we were devising solutions that had no relationship to the problem or they were so unrelated. The problem is we need more sales. Okay. But why didn't you make sales? Well, we didn't make sales. We need more sales. I mean, why, they doesn't make any sense. So you have to get that texture of this happened qualitatively. This is what happened. And now, what do I as an intelligent human being think might have been the problem? If somebody is on your page for 10 minutes and go, all, you know, they go through all that content, whatever, and they get to the end and they don't buy, there's a probably a strong signal. This is a price issue, unless prices are late or what, I'm just making this up. But it's pretty strong signal. You really had their attention, but maybe that price was just way out of their league, which can tell you about the quality of your traffic and everything. But they've been there for 10 minutes. They still didn't buy. I'd love to retarget those people. And I would love to show them a different way. And maybe, you know, the specific question, maybe you're trying to sell with video. And if you retarget to a written sales page is better. Maybe you're trying to sell with like a hardcore direct response benefits focused offer page, maybe your audience doesn't respond well to that. And maybe when you retarget, you retarget to something so much softer, which different approach, maybe a narrative approach or something. And generally, there are all kinds of nuances here. So yes, when people don't buy, retargeting them to reengage. And because they've raised their hand already, they got almost all the way there. There's very high quality. But don't do that without really doing some excavating first. Figure out, ask yourself, why didn't these people buy? And don't settle for the first answer that comes to mind. Keep writing stuff down. If they're on your email list, and you can see who it was, send them an email. Reach out. Don't be weird about it. I'm like, listen, I noticed you were interested in this thing. And this isn't some weird automated thing. But a lot of people have gone to that and they didn't, they didn't buy and I want this to be accessible. So would you mind sharing why, you know, what about it? What could I have done better? And if someone really gives you great stuff, give you a give them. So don't don't use this as a weird way to like offer a discount code. Like that's not what we're doing here. You want, I can't stress this enough, you want the answer to the question, or the answers to the question. Why is this happening? And then engineer the solution to address the what you uncover. Don't just create solutions looking for problems. That makes sense. You want to go to the next one, Lauren? Sure. Let me see. So one quick question that Cohen had was, he set the Facebook ads conversion to purchase. But purchase in my custom events means that they get to page two of our six page MPPS. Is this a good way to go? I would not recommend that. I would recommend if you use a purchase event or a lead event or something like that, actually make it a lead or a purchase. If you're doing like a click to page two, you could make that a custom event, or you could just have it be a page view event that is a custom conversion. But generally, don't try to get cutesy with Facebook like that, where you're firing events when something's not happening, because it'll come back to bite you. Facebook is getting a picture of all those people who click to page two as purchasers, and that's really not accurate. And then it's just better to work with the algorithm in that way. But then you can, the others, Sean. I just want to connect something that you just said to clarify for everybody. Facebook has a body of data that says, this person does this thing that we agree is a purchase, they buy something. If you call something else that it doesn't make it that, it just confuses the algorithm. You're basically saying, we're going to call something a purchase that leads to a purchase that's not a purchase, but send me people who do this other thing. It's a mismatch, so don't do that. And I guess two last questions we'll hit quickly from Cohen again. So how can we apply this in our Dutch relationship coaching niche? That's your specialty, Lauren. Perfect. We will just talk about how you're a Dutch relationship coach. I have a Dutch relationship coach. Most couples want to, they won't have this in their relationships, but that's a broad desire. And they normally talk most about communication problems, but the real problems are deeper. Then you have the couples that have real problems and almost are on the verge of divorce. We can deliver the magic so that couples in just two days are back in love again and have all the tools to go and grow from there. But how do we connect this with the needs that they want? I guess what I would say is, I've written for the marriage niche before. Sean and I actually worked with a marriage counselor. And this goes for any customer. You just determine their needs and then you, how does your product or service meet those needs? A lot of times the difficulty with marriage is they don't want to face the reality that maybe they need counseling, maybe they need better communication, maybe they need what they really need. They just want to fix their partner. So anyway, for whatever that's worth, if you can somehow, you'll need to communicate in a way that actually resonates with them, because sometimes they don't want to face the truth of what they truly need. But Sean, what are your thoughts? I'm trying not to laugh out loud. I've been married 24 years. This idea of fixing your partner, to me, it's kind of analogous, was it Mark Twain said? There are some lessons in life you can only learn by doing them, like picking up a cat by his tail. Trying to fix your partner relationship probably is analogous to picking up a cat by its tail, that you very quickly learn why that was not a good idea. Obviously, we've been doing this a long call if I'm laughing at stuff like this. So I think the answer that Lauren said is exactly the answer here, which is, in how you do it, it really depends on, and I think you can do it multiple ways to hit different parts of your audience. So one segment of your audience would like to see their desired future reality presented to them and then have you explain how you get them there. That's a very common future pacing. You're about a lot that you describe for them what they, in their heart, they want most from the perspective of their relationship. And you do you dimensionalize that emotionally and really you describe that to them in very genuine terms. This is not a trick, very genuine terms. And then you work backwards, you've shown them what they want and then you explain how you get them there. You could also flip that around and you could focus on what maybe for some part of your audience is the most immediate thing, which is they're seeing the problem. Like this is what's happening and then connect it. And this is what happened, but this is what you really want. And then we know how to get you from where you are to where you're going. It's another way to approach it. And there are lots of things in between and there are lots of ways to deal with dialogue. You can make the first two lines of the ad that people see be the phrase that they are hearing either in their head or their spouse say. That's really impactful and that might grab their attention. There are lots of ways to go about doing this, but what they all have in common is that you are getting to the core of what they want most. That's marketing, that's selling, that's everything. And it is so easy. And this is Seth Godin comments about it's much easier to we're not we're not in a persuading business. We're directing its interest and desire already. We're just we're making a channel for it. We're not trying to create it. And I think I'm a Seth Godin. I love this phrase. Like if you open a nudist colony, sell to people who are already nudists. Don't try to convince people to become nudists. That's marketing and sales. So what we get really good at that is when we get the skill to have the patience to listen and to be present and to hear what our audience really wants and then to move heaven and earth to organize our offer to give them exactly what it is that they want. That's that's the name of the game. All right. Last question from Kevin. I was right, Kevin. We saved your question to last. It's not the question that we thought. Okay. Quick follow up to the agency's very influence question. Would you focus on SEO or paid traffic to get people to see it? So I would focus on paid traffic for a couple of reasons. One of which is the whole reason that I created the traffic and is because it's I have some level of control over it. I think too much of SEO to me still feels like voodoo black magic. I don't it just doesn't make any sense. Yeah, I know it works. I don't know how it works. It doesn't seem to work super consistently. But the more nuanced answer to your question is I want to and this is something that Andre and I do very deliberately. I think we enjoy doing and I perhaps enjoy it to a fault. But when we when we teach, if you if you take a step back, if you read our emails, if you see how the series is are organized, if you see that you have to actually actively engage to go farther, like if you when you look at all of that, you'll also notice that when we're teaching, we never stray from what we teach that what we teach is a an expression of what we believe. And and that's not it's not a trick. It's who we are. We can't do it any other way. So what I what I I mentioned that because if I were whatever agency model I created would be a how I got my customers would be a demonstration of my of what I was selling. Because I want to be able to subtly reinforce when someone says, you know, how do we know what you do works? Say simple, how did how did you hear about me? Well, we did x y and z right. That's what I do. That's why you're here. I want to be able to have that level of conversation. So if I have an SEO agency, I'm not going to do a paid ad to get people. It's like, well, wait a minute, why do you pay for traffic? When you're an SEO specialist, like I don't want to have that conversation. I want it to be congruent. So whenever my specialty is, is going to be how I get my customers assuming that's possible. If there isn't a direct analog, if it's not in the traffic thing, I want it to be as close as possible. So if I only work with a and this was true for me, I've been true for a very long time. If if I only work with clients who are referred to me by a trusted person, someone I know, a close person who is vouching for the client, if I only do that, then I want my lead generation to, or I want my whole business to be congruent with that. And I don't want to have something that distracts from that. So I don't want to say to somebody, I'm going to take your call because my buddy knows us as you're great. And then they see my ads all over Facebook and they're like, well, I'm pretty sure you take my call anyway, because your ads are all over Facebook. Like that's not congruent. So whatever I offered for a, for the service, I would want to be a mirror image of what I was offering to the client if that made sense. That's the real answer to the question.
